Environmental exposures including air pollution, heat, and heavy metals (arsenic, cadmium, chromium, lead, mercury) are strongly associated with spontaneous preterm labor, with heavy metals promoting oxidative stress, epigenetic modifications, inflammation, and decidual cell death. Dietary factors also play a critical role: high-fat diets increase preterm labor risk through inflammation and gut dysbiosis, while Mediterranean-style diets and dietary selenium are protective; additionally, specific heavy metals like copper elevate preterm risk by increasing cholesterol and triglycerides, whereas zinc and selenium offer protective effects. At the molecular level, inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α drive preterm birth through GPR91-mediated signaling, while progesterone-regulated pathways involving microRNAs (miR-200a, miR-199a-3p) and gaseous mediators like hydrogen sulfide promote uterine quiescence and inhibit contraction-associated proteins. Genetic variants in genes such as EBF1, EEFSEC, AGTR2, and titin, combined with sociodemographic factors (maternal age, race, stress) and pregnancy complications (kidney dysfunction, endometriosis history), further increase preterm birth susceptibility.

title: Dysregulation of microRNA (miRNA) Due to Phthalate/Phthalate Metabolite Exposure and Associated Health Effects: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Phthalates and their metabolites function as endocrine disruptors that dysregulate microRNA (miRNA) expression, predominantly through upregulation, triggering widespread adverse health effects including reproductive dysfunction, endometriosis, oncogenesis, metabolic disorders, and neurodevelopmental impairment. Exposure to specific phthalate metabolites—including MEHP, DEHP, and others—induces dysregulation of distinct miRNAs (miR-34a, miR-15b, miR-141, miR-184, miR-125, miR-let-7, and others) that modulate critical reproductive pathways involving oxidative stress, apoptosis, and angiogenesis. For instance, MEHP upregulates oxidative-stress-responsive miRNAs including miR-155-5p and miR-126-3p, while DEHP suppresses follicular development by upregulating ovarian miRNAs that inhibit KITL expression and impair granulosa cell proliferation and oocyte maturation. These dysregulated miRNAs regulate key genes such as NRG1 and ABI2, which are implicated in reproductive system diseases and organismal abnormalities. The evidence indicates that phthalate-induced miRNA dysregulation represents a central epigenetic mechanism linking environmental chemical exposure to endometriosis and other reproductive pathologies.

title: Impact of endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) on epigenetic regulation in the uterus: a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines how endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—including bisphenol A, phthalates, dioxins, polychlorinated biphenyls, diethylstilbestrol, and nonylphenol—disrupt epigenetic regulation in the uterus and cause multiple reproductive pathologies. EDCs interfere with the endocrine system and induce epigenetic modulations through three primary mechanisms: altered DNA methylation patterns, modified histone marks, and microRNA alterations, which collectively dysregulate gene expression. The resulting gene expression changes drive various uterine and reproductive disorders, including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, atypical endometrial hyperplasia, female infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, menstrual irregularities, and increased tumorigenesis risk. Mechanistically, specific EDCs activate signaling pathways such as PI3K/AKT and modulate key epigenetic regulators including EZH2, TET2, HOXA10, and ASCL2, with bisphenol A demonstrating particularly potent effects on endometrial cancer cell proliferation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and inflammatory gene upregulation. These findings underscore how environmental chemical exposures during critical developmental windows trigger persistent epigenetic changes that compromise uterine health and female reproductive outcomes.

title: Causal validation of the relationship between air pollution and lung cancer: A bidirectional Mendelian randomization study and meta-analysis
summary: This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study establishes a causal relationship between nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) exposure and lung cancer, with NO₂ demonstrating stronger causal evidence than broader air pollution categories. The mechanistic pathways involve NO₂-induced oxidative stress and chronic inflammation through upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α, coupled with direct DNA damage, genetic mutations, and chromosomal instability that activate oncogenic pathways (KRAS, EGFR) while inhibiting tumor suppressors like TP53. Additionally, NO₂ disrupts mitochondrial function and alters epigenetic regulation through DNA methylation and histone modification, processes that collectively promote tumor initiation and progression. The study identifies interactive effects between NO₂ and co-pollutants such as PM2.5 and ozone, which synergistically exacerbate oxidative damage and inflammatory responses. Long-term NO₂ exposure also associates with multiple respiratory diseases including asthma, bronchitis, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and epidemiological evidence suggests associations with hormone-sensitive cancers (breast, ovarian, uterine), highlighting the pleiotropic health effects of air pollution beyond pulmonary malignancy.

title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances in Eutopic Endometrium Tissue and Risk of Endometriosis: Findings from the Investigating Mixtures of Pollutants and Endometriosis in Tissue (IMPLANT) Study
summary: The IMPLANT Study investigated per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in eutopic endometrial tissue from individuals with and without endometriosis, given that PFAS are widespread environmental contaminants linked to gynecologic disease. Five PFAS compounds—PFOS, PFOA, PFHxS, PFNA, and PFOSA—were detected in nearly all eutopic endometrial tissue samples, with higher concentrations found in ectopic compared to eutopic tissue. Although individual PFAS were not associated with overall endometriosis diagnosis, PFOS, PFOSA, and PFHxS in eutopic tissue showed significantly elevated risk for advanced-stage disease (ASRM stages 3–4 versus 1–2), and the overall PFAS mixture was positively associated with disease stage. These findings suggest that PFAS contribute to endometriosis pathology through multiple mechanisms, including disruption of estrogen and progesterone signaling, induction of oxidative stress, and promotion of inflammation. The moderate correlations between serum and tissue PFAS levels support tissue accumulation as a relevant exposure metric for assessing endometriosis severity, highlighting PFAS as potential environmental risk factors for advanced endometriosis.

title: Integrated multi-omics analysis identifies TLR4-mediated mechanisms in ATBC-induced ovarian dysfunction and female infertility: A network toxicology, transcriptomic, and Mendelian randomization study
summary: This integrative multi-omics study identifies TLR4 as a critical mediator of reproductive toxicity induced by acetyl tributyl citrate (ATBC), a widely used plasticizer associated with ovarian dysfunction and female infertility. Molecular docking revealed strong binding between ATBC and TLR4, along with the estrogen receptor ESR1, suggesting direct molecular interactions. Network analysis pinpointed 19 core targets in ATBC-induced ovarian damage (TNF, SRC, BCL2) and 30 targets in infertility pathways (TNF, SRC, CASP3), with significant enrichment in apoptosis, oxidative stress response, and PI3K-AKT signaling. Notably, Mendelian randomization analysis paradoxically indicated TLR4 as protective against infertility, suggesting complex immunological roles. The study reveals that environmental toxins broadly disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, reduce egg quality, impair the uterine environment, and induce chronic inflammation and oxidative stress that inhibit follicular maturation and oocyte development. Related environmental pollutants including phthalates are associated with polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and premature ovarian failure, establishing ATBC and similar plasticizers as significant reproductive hazards through multiple interconnected molecular pathways.

title: Potential effects of environmental toxicants on sperm quality and potential risk for fertility in humans
summary:
# Summary

Environmental toxicants significantly compromise male fertility through multiple mechanisms affecting spermatogenesis and reproductive hormone regulation. Air pollutants including particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10), sulfur dioxide, nitrogen dioxide, and ozone directly reduce sperm parameters such as count, motility, and morphology while increasing sperm DNA fragmentation. Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) like bisphenol A (BPA), organochlorinated pesticides, and polychlorinated biphenyls interfere with the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis by blocking steroid hormone signaling, reducing testosterone production, and suppressing gonadotropin release from Leydig and Sertoli cells. Emerging pollutants such as polystyrene microplastics and nanoplastics accumulate in testicular tissue, disrupting the blood-testis barrier through oxidative stress and downregulating critical steroidogenic proteins, thereby impairing spermatogenesis and fertility. Notably, impaired semen parameters serve as a biological marker for broader systemic health dysfunction, including cardiovascular and metabolic disease. These findings underscore environmental pollution as a major contributor to rising male infertility rates and suggest that reproductive health assessment may predict long-term health outcomes.

title: Smoking in primary headaches – a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates differential associations between smoking and primary headache types. Current smoking significantly increases migraine risk (OR = 1.29), yet paradoxically decreases tension-type headache (TTH) risk (OR = 0.78), while no association was found with cluster headache or migraine with aura. Notably, smoking prevalence varies substantially across headache types, with cluster headache patients showing the highest smoking prevalence at 65%, compared to 20% in migraine and 19% in TTH, suggesting different mechanisms of action depending on headache phenotype. The opposing effects on migraine versus TTH risk may reflect distinct pathophysiological pathways, with nicotine's impact on vascular health and neurological function potentially explaining the variable responses. These findings underscore smoking as an important environmental and lifestyle factor in primary headache etiology, though the mechanism underlying the protective effect against TTH remains unclear and warrants further investigation.

title: The Role of Heavy Metals in the Biology of Female Cancers
summary:
# Summary

Heavy metals including cadmium, lead, mercury, and arsenic promote carcinogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms relevant to hormone-responsive cancers. These metals function as "metalloestrogens" by directly interacting with estrogen receptor-alpha (ERα), which is upregulated in approximately 75% of breast cancers and drives proliferation through estrogen-mediated signaling. Additionally, heavy metals induce oxidative stress by generating excessive reactive oxygen species, leading to DNA damage, genomic instability, and impaired DNA repair mechanisms. In experimental models, cadmium and arsenite activate ERα and downstream proliferative pathways including p38/MAPK, ERK1/2, and AKT signaling, while also upregulating estrogen-responsive genes such as progesterone receptor, GREB1, c-fos, and cyclin D1. Notably, environmentally relevant exposure levels of these metals significantly increased mammary tumor incidence in animal models and accelerated developmental milestones in female offspring through ERα activation. The protective estrogen receptor-beta isoform counteracts these effects by downregulating ERα activity and inhibiting pro-tumorigenic pathways, highlighting the critical balance between estrogen receptor subtypes in cancer susceptibility to heavy metal exposure.

title: Phthalate Metabolites and Their Relationship with Abdominal and General Obesity: Evidence from the Aragon Workers’ Health Study (AWHS)
summary:
# Summary

Phthalates are endocrine-disrupting chemicals ubiquitously present in plastic products and food packaging that demonstrate significant associations with obesity phenotypes in adults. This study of Spanish workers found that urinary concentrations of di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) and its primary metabolites—mono-(2-ethyl-5-oxohexyl) phthalate (MEOHP) and mono-(2-ethyl-5-hydroxyhexyl) phthalate (MEHHP)—were positively associated with general obesity, while MEOHP and related metabolites also showed dose-dependent associations with abdominal obesity. The biological mechanisms underlying phthalate-induced obesity likely involve anti-androgenic effects that reduce testosterone levels, impaired energy metabolism, elevated leptin signaling, and disrupted fatty acid metabolism in adipose tissue. Notably, phthalate exposure has been linked not only to obesity and metabolic syndrome but also to type 2 diabetes and endometriosis, establishing it as a pleiotropic environmental risk factor with widespread metabolic consequences. These findings underscore the importance of understanding how widespread chemical exposures influence obesity development and related endocrine disorders, particularly given the near-universal human exposure to phthalates through dietary and consumer product pathways.

title: A State of the Science Review of Human Health Effects of the Michigan Polybrominated Biphenyl Contamination after Five Decades
summary:
# Summary

Polybrominated biphenyls (PBBs) and brominated flame retardants represent potent endocrine-disrupting chemicals that cause widespread reproductive and developmental toxicity through activation of nuclear receptors, steroid hormone receptors, and the aryl hydrocarbon receptor. A 1978 contamination of Michigan's food supply with PBBs resulted in extensive human exposure via adipose tissue, serum, and breast milk, with documented effects including accelerated pubertal development and earlier menarche in exposed girls, urogenital problems and delayed puberty in boys, increased miscarriage risk in subsequent generations, and altered reproductive hormone levels including reduced follicle-stimulating hormone and estrone 3-glucuronide. Beyond reproductive effects, PBBs and related brominated flame retardants cause increased breast cancer risk, thyroid dysfunction, metabolic abnormalities, and epigenetic alterations affecting estrogenic and immune pathways. The bioaccumulation of these chemicals in adipose tissue, combined with impaired elimination rates in individuals with higher body mass index and greater pregnancy history, suggests persistent multigenerational exposure risks that may contribute to conditions like endometriosis through chronic endocrine disruption and altered immune responses.

title: Di (2-ethylhexyl) Phthalate decrease pregnancy rate via disrupting the microbe-gut-hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis in mice
summary: Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), an environmental pollutant, disrupts the microbe-gut-hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis through multiple interconnected mechanisms to reduce pregnancy rates in mice. DEHP exposure alters gut microbiota composition, reducing bacterial richness and diversity while shifting the abundance of major phyla, particularly decreasing Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes. The chemical activates hypothalamic astrocytes and upregulates neuregulin-1 (NRG1), which binds to astrocyte ErbB2 receptors and triggers phosphorylation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, ultimately promoting prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) and gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) release. Elevated PGE2 and aberrant GnRH signaling impair ovarian function and disrupt normal hormone cycles, leading to follicle atresia and reduced litter sizes in both short- and long-term exposures. The negative correlation between serum PGE2 levels and pregnancy rate, combined with DEHP's tissue accumulation in the intestine, blood, and hypothalamus, demonstrates a direct causal relationship between environmental chemical exposure and reproductive dysfunction via the gut-brain-reproductive axis.

title: Filling Gaps in Population Estimates of Phthalate Exposure Globally: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of International Biomonitoring Data
journal: International journal of hygiene and environmental health
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis of international biomonitoring data reveals widespread global exposure to phthalates, ubiquitous plasticizer chemicals that function as endocrine-disrupting compounds. Phthalates are strongly associated with reproductive toxicity, reduced fertility, oxidative stress, and testicular damage, while long-term exposure has been linked to type II diabetes, neurodevelopmental impairment, obesity, cancer, and cardiovascular mortality. The analysis identified significant geographic disparities, with populations in the Middle East and South Asia exhibiting up to 10-fold higher phthalate metabolite concentrations than other regions, while youth populations showed markedly elevated exposures compared to pregnant women who demonstrated the lowest concentrations. Temporal trends reveal divergent patterns: metabolites of the plasticizer DEHP and some alternative phthalates (MEP, MBzP) have declined over time, likely reflecting regulatory restrictions, whereas DBP metabolites have increased exponentially internationally. These findings underscore the persistence of phthalate contamination as a global public health concern with substantial implications for reproductive and metabolic health outcomes.

title: Association between Perfluorooctanoic Acid–Related Poor Embryo Quality and Metabolite Alterations in Human Follicular Fluid during IVF: A Cohort Study
summary: This cohort study demonstrates that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), particularly perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), accumulate in follicular fluid and significantly impair reproductive outcomes during in vitro fertilization. Multiple PFAS compounds including PFOA, PFDA, PFHpS, PFNA, and n-PFOS reduce both high-quality embryo rates and clinical pregnancy outcomes, with PFOA being the dominant contributor to these adverse effects. The mechanisms underlying PFAS-related reproductive toxicity involve disruption of follicular development and ovulation, along with substantial alterations in follicular fluid metabolites—specifically decreased levels of organonitrogens (proline-tryptophan and lauryldimethylamine oxide) and sphingolipids (phytosphingosine, N-myristoylsphinganine, and N-lauroyl-D-erythro-sphinganine) that normally correlate with higher embryo quality. Causal mediation analysis reveals that poor embryo quality serves as a critical mechanistic link between PFAS exposure and reduced clinical pregnancy outcomes, suggesting that PFAS-induced metabolic disruptions directly compromise oocyte and embryo developmental competence. These findings underscore PFAS as endocrine disruptors with significant implications for female reproductive health and fertility, particularly relevant to environmental exposures in populations at risk for reproductive disorders including endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Estimation of Dermal Exposure to Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) from Feminine Hygiene Products: Integrating Measurement Data and Physiologically Based Toxicokinetic (PBTK) Model
summary:
# Summary

Feminine hygiene products contain multiple volatile organic compounds (VOCs)—including benzene, p-cymene, n-nonane, hexane, and methyl ethyl ketone—added intentionally as fragrances or present as production residues. Using physiologically based toxicokinetic modeling, this study found that dermal exposure to these VOCs from feminine hygiene products causes mucosal and skin irritation, liver and kidney damage, and poses both cancer and noncancer health risks, with benzene from moisturizers presenting notable carcinogenic potential and n-nonane from wash products posing noncancer risks. The VOCs are rapidly absorbed through the skin and preferentially accumulate in adipose tissue (1-4 orders of magnitude higher than other tissues) due to their lipophilic properties, with p-cymene and n-nonane showing concerning cumulative trends across menstrual cycles. While the majority of absorbed VOCs undergo hepatic metabolism or exhalation, the study demonstrates that feminine wash products contribute most substantially to overall VOC exposure (84.1%), followed by pads for certain compounds like methylene chloride and toluene. These findings indicate that long-term dermal exposure to VOCs from feminine hygiene products, though often at low individual levels, results in systemic distribution and potential health risks warranting further investigation and regulatory attention.

title: Associations of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances in follicular fluid with polycystic ovarian syndrome in infertile women may be mediated by sex hormones
summary: This study demonstrates that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), which accumulate in follicular fluid, are associated with increased polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) risk in infertile women, with this association potentially mediated by disrupted sex hormone signaling. Multiple PFAS compounds including perfluoro-n-hexanoic acid, PFOA, and PFOS were detected in follicular fluid at measurable concentrations and showed distinct associations with reproductive hormones: some PFAS negatively correlated with luteinizing hormone while others positively associated with estradiol in patient samples. In cell-based experiments, PFOA, PFOS, and perfluoro-n-heptanoic acid exposure reduced estradiol production and granulosa cell viability in a dose-dependent manner. Mechanistic analysis revealed that luteinizing hormone explained approximately 19.4% of the association between perfluoro-n-hexanoic acid exposure and PCOS, implicating steroid hormone dysregulation as a key pathway linking PFAS exposure to PCOS pathogenesis. The findings suggest that endocrine-disrupting properties of PFAS may contribute to PCOS development through alterations in the follicular microenvironment and hormone synthesis, supporting the hypothesis that environmental chemical exposures represent modifiable risk factors for reproductive disorders in infertile populations.

title: Prenatal Bisphenol B Exposure Induces Adult Male Offspring Reproductive Dysfunction via ERα Inhibition-Triggered MHC I-Mediated Testicular Immunological Responses
summary:
# Summary

Prenatal exposure to bisphenol B (BPB) impairs reproductive function in male offspring through a mechanistic pathway involving estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) inhibition and subsequent testicular immune dysregulation. BPB binds to and suppresses ERα expression in the testis, which triggers marked upregulation of MHC class I molecules and downstream activation of macrophages, CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and pro-inflammatory cytokine production. This immunological cascade causes substantial structural and functional damage to the testis, including reduced seminiferous epithelium thickness, decreased sperm count and testosterone production, and diminished Leydig cell populations. The study also found detectable BPB levels in maternal serum and placental tissues, with BPB detected in a subset of endometriosis patients, suggesting potential environmental exposure relevance to both male and female reproductive disorders. These findings establish a novel developmental origin for BPB-induced male reproductive dysfunction mediated by ERα-dependent immune activation rather than direct gonadal toxicity.

title: Oral exposure to di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate alters the expression of GATA6, OCT4, and CDX2 in blastocysts and decreases the rate of implantation in a mouse model
journal: Toxicological sciences : an official journal of the Society of Toxicology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that oral exposure to di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) severely compromises early embryonic development and implantation in mice through multiple mechanisms. At doses of 200 and 2,000 μg/kg/day, DEHP downregulates critical transcription factors—GATA6 and OCT4, which are essential for inner cell mass formation, and CDX2, which governs trophectoderm development—leading to impaired blastocyst quality and reduced numbers of developmentally advanced blastocysts. The chemical induces oocyte and embryo fragmentation, developmental arrest, and decreased inner cell mass and trophectoderm quality, particularly at the highest dose. Both embryonic exposure to DEHP and maternal uterine exposure independently reduce implantation rates, indicating that DEHP impacts both the developing embryo and the receptive uterine environment. These findings link environmental phthalate exposure to reproductive pathologies including reduced embryo quality, implantation failure, and miscarriage, with dysregulation of Nrf2-Notch1 and Nf2 signaling pathways likely mediating the suppression of pluripotency markers.

title: The Silent Threat of BPA: Its Pervasive Presence and Impact on Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

Bisphenol A (BPA), a ubiquitous endocrine-disrupting chemical found in polycarbonate plastics, food and beverage cans, and thermal paper, exerts widespread reproductive toxicity by binding to estrogen receptors and mimicking estrogenic signaling. In females, BPA disrupts ovarian function through multiple mechanisms including suppression of genes involved in steroid hormone synthesis, interference with follicle growth and maturation, induction of oxidative stress and inflammation, and epigenetic modifications that alter gene expression patterns—collectively resulting in irregular menstrual cycles, ovulation impairment, and increased risk of endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome. In males, BPA exposure compromises testicular development and spermatogenesis while reducing steroid hormone synthesis and inducing toxicity in reproductive accessory glands including the epididymis, prostate, and seminal vesicles. BPA-induced oxidative stress causes direct damage to reproductive tissues, and accumulating evidence suggests exposure during critical developmental windows may trigger transgenerational reproductive effects. Mitigation strategies include reducing consumption of canned foods, using glass or ceramic containers, selecting BPA-free products, and maintaining a diet rich in antioxidants, with quercetin supplementation showing promise in ameliorating BPA-induced reproductive toxicity.

title: An assessment of maternal concentrations of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and trace elements during the second trimester of pregnancy in western Pennsylvania
summary: This study assessed maternal exposure to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and trace elements during the second trimester of pregnancy in western Pennsylvania, finding that both chemical classes are ubiquitously detected in pregnant women and can cross the placenta to reach the fetus. Trace elements including arsenic, cadmium, and lead, as well as PFAS compounds like PFOS and PFOA, were detected in the majority of study participants, with concentrations of multiple trace elements elevated in pregnant women compared to non-pregnant populations. Tobacco smoke exposure significantly increased maternal concentrations of several trace elements and PFAS, including cadmium, lead, and other metals, with smoking participants showing notably higher levels of arsenic, cesium, molybdenum, tin, and thallium. The study demonstrates that both trace elements and PFAS are associated with multiple adverse reproductive and developmental outcomes, including spontaneous abortion, preeclampsia, preterm birth, low birth weight, abnormal organ development, and infant mortality. Additionally, these environmental exposures were linked to reproductive conditions in women of reproductive age including endometriosis, infertility, and polycystic ovary syndrome. These findings highlight the critical importance of monitoring environmental chemical exposures during pregnancy to protect fetal development and maternal reproductive health.

title: Association of urine parabens with liver function in US adolescents based on NHANES 2007–2016
summary:
# Summary

This study examined associations between urinary paraben exposure and liver function markers in US adolescents using NHANES data from 2007–2016. Parabens, widely used preservatives in cosmetic and personal care products, showed significant but weak associations with altered hepatic biomarkers including reduced albumin and alkaline phosphatase alongside elevated total bilirubin and gamma-glutamyl transferase. Methyl paraben, propyl paraben, and butyl paraben demonstrated the most consistent effects, with butyl paraben showing the strongest associations with liver function changes. Beyond hepatic effects, parabens exert endocrine-disrupting properties by binding estrogen and androgen receptors, potentially affecting reproductive and thyroid hormone levels and compromising developmental homeostasis. The findings suggest that cumulative paraben exposure from cosmetics and personal care products may adversely affect hepatic metabolism and endocrine function during the critical adolescent period, highlighting the need for regulatory assessment of these ubiquitous environmental chemicals.

title: Impact of prenatal Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate exposure on pubertal development in female offspring rats: A focus on ERα-Mediated IGF-1/NKB crosstalk in the hypothalamus
summary:
# Summary

Prenatal exposure to the environmental contaminant di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) precipitates precocious puberty in female rat offspring through disruption of hypothalamic estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) signaling. DEHP upregulates the IGF-1 and neurokinin B (NKB) pathways in the hypothalamus, where ERα serves as a critical regulator of both systems; these pathways converge to stimulate GnRH secretion and accelerate the pubertal timeline. The study demonstrates that IGF-1 and NKB exhibit synergistic interactions in upregulating NK3R expression and jointly promote growth hormone and prolactin secretion, key drivers of pubertal progression. Pharmacological inhibition of ERα, IGF-1 receptor, or NK3R signaling each prevented DEHP-induced precocious puberty, confirming the central role of this ERα-mediated IGF-1/NKB crosstalk mechanism. Beyond reproductive effects, prenatal DEHP exposure was associated with broader developmental toxicity including growth restriction, ovarian dysfunction, endometrial ectopia, reduced fertility, obesity, metabolic dysfunction, and neurodevelopmental impairments including reduced white matter volume and cognitive deficits.

title: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and epigenetic regulation in embryonic development: Mechanisms, impacts, and emerging trends
summary: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) such as bisphenol A (BPA), phthalates, and DDT disrupt embryonic development by interfering with endocrine equilibrium and altering epigenetic processes through multiple mechanisms. These chemicals mimic natural hormones like estrogen and interact with nuclear receptors including estrogen receptors and PPARγ, thereby disrupting normal gene regulation and causing reproductive toxicity, developmental neurotoxicity, and other adverse health effects. EDCs alter epigenetic machinery by disrupting DNA methylation patterns—BPA upregulates miRNA-29 to suppress DNMTs (DNMT1, DNMT3a, DNMT3b), while vinclozolin increases DNMT expression through androgen receptor signaling—ultimately leading to aberrant silencing or activation of developmental genes. The epigenetic regulation of embryonic development relies on coordinated histone modifications (activating H3K4me3 and H3K27ac marks; silencing H3K27me3 and H3K9me3 marks), DNA methylation via DNMT enzymes, and noncoding RNAs (miRNAs, lncRNAs) that control approximately 30% of protein-coding genes. Emerging therapeutic approaches employ DNMT inhibitors to reverse EDC-induced epigenetic changes and restore normal gene expression patterns. This review highlights how even low-dose EDC exposures during critical developmental windows can cause lasting epigenetic alterations that perturb reproductive, developmental, and neurological processes.

title: Microfluidic paper–based analytical extraction devices (µPAEDs): a cost-effective and portable solution for biomarkers, contaminants and VOC detection
summary:
# Summary

Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (µPADs) represent a cost-effective and portable platform for detecting biomarkers and environmental chemical contaminants across multiple biological matrices including blood, plasma, sweat, and saliva. Environmental exposures to pesticides and other chemical contaminants are associated with significant health effects including endocrine disruption, neurological disorders, and asthma, making their detection clinically relevant. µPADs have been successfully applied to detect diverse analytes such as pesticides, proteins, and hormones through various mechanisms enhanced by nanomaterials including graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles that improve device sensitivity and electron transfer. The devices can also detect oxidative stress biomarkers such as 3-nitrotyrosine and 4-nitroquinoline-N-oxide, as well as cancer-associated markers like carcinoembryonic antigen. This technology offers a practical diagnostic approach for identifying chemical exposures and associated biomarkers that may contribute to environmental health complications in clinical populations.

title: Improving efficacy and safety of surgery in benign gynaecology: the case for indocyanine green
summary:
# Summary

Indocyanine green (ICG), a near-infrared fluorescent dye, offers significant improvements in surgical safety and efficacy for benign gynecological procedures, particularly in endometriosis management. When administered intravenously, ICG undergoes rapid hepatic clearance and emits infrared fluorescence under near-infrared illumination, enabling real-time visualization of critical anatomical structures and tissue perfusion. ICG demonstrates superior performance compared to traditional alternatives like methylene blue and cystoscopy, facilitating detection of endometriotic implants, identification and vascularity assessment of the ureter and bladder, evaluation of bowel and ovarian perfusion, and determination of anastomotic viability during complex bowel resection for endometriosis. In meta-analyses, ICG use has been associated with significantly higher bilateral sentinel lymph node detection rates and reduced anastomotic leak rates in bowel surgery. Despite rare adverse effects including anaphylaxis and urticaria primarily in patients with iodide allergies, ICG is generally recognized as a safe, non-toxic substance approved by international regulatory bodies, making it a valuable tool for reducing complications such as bladder injury, ureteric injury, and bowel ischemia in complex endometriosis surgery.

title: Arsenic in Water and Food: Toxicity and Human Exposure
summary:
# Summary

Inorganic arsenic (iAs), a human carcinogen found predominantly in drinking water, groundwater, rice, and seafood, causes widespread toxicity across multiple organ systems including the endocrine, reproductive, cardiovascular, neurological, and immune systems. The inorganic form is substantially more toxic than organic arsenic, with trivalent arsenic species (particularly MMA(III)) being more cytotoxic than pentavalent forms due to their ability to inhibit mitochondrial function and generate reactive oxygen and nitrogen species that cause DNA damage and impair DNA repair mechanisms. Chronic exposure to iAs induces numerous pathologies including diabetes mellitus, liver and kidney dysfunction, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive impairment, and reproductive complications, while also driving cancer development through multiple mechanisms including oxidative stress, DNA damage, and microRNA dysregulation. Prenatal exposure presents particular concern, as iAs readily crosses the placental barrier and causes abortion, fetal death, premature birth, and low birth weight, with postnatal exposure during infancy causing immune suppression and developmental impairment. The identified adverse effects on the endocrine and reproductive systems are particularly relevant to endometriosis pathogenesis, as environmental chemical exposures are recognized contributors to disease development and progression.

title: An Analysis of the Role of Bisphenol A in Breast and Reproductive-System Cancers
summary:
# Summary

Bisphenol A (BPA), an endocrine-disrupting chemical ubiquitous in food containers, polycarbonates, and household dust, is strongly associated with reproductive system and breast cancers through its estrogen-mimicking properties. BPA exerts its carcinogenic effects primarily by binding to estrogen receptors (ERα, ERβ, GPR30, and GPER) and inhibiting thyroid hormone receptors, thereby dysregulating estrogen signaling pathways and promoting estrogen-dependent malignancies including breast, endometrial, and ovarian cancers. Serum BPA concentrations were significantly elevated in reproductive system cancer patients compared to controls, with dietary intake—particularly heating food in plastic, storing food in plastic packaging, and consuming bottled water—identified as the primary exposure route. Women with cancer showed higher mean BPA levels than healthy individuals, and behavioral analyses revealed that reproductive system cancer patients more frequently engaged in practices associated with BPA exposure, such as consuming fast food and using plastic containers for food storage. The study supports the hypothesis that internal BPA burden is linked to hormone-dependent malignancies, emphasizing the importance of reducing exposure through dietary and behavioral modifications.

title: Association between endocrine disrupting chemicals and female infertility: a study based on NHANES database
summary:
# Summary

This NHANES database study demonstrates that exposure to multiple classes of endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) is significantly associated with female infertility. Multiple chemical metabolites—including phthalates (DnBP, DEHP, DiNP, DEHTP), perfluorinated compounds (PFOA, PFUS, PFOS, PFHxS), and phytoestrogens (equol)—showed strong positive correlations with infertility risk, with DnBP demonstrating the highest odds ratios. EDCs exert their reproductive effects through disruption of cellular signaling pathways, particularly estrogen signaling, as well as through specific mechanisms such as PFOS-mediated inhibition of oocyte maturation and DEHP metabolite-induced reductions in oocyte quality and quantity. Long-term reproductive damage from EDC exposure was evident in EDC-exposed women being up to six times more likely to be menopausal than non-exposed women. Age and elevated BMI emerged as critical effect modifiers, with women aged 35 and older or with BMI ≥24 showing substantially elevated infertility risks associated with EDC exposure, suggesting that cumulative chemical burden and reduced reproductive reserve compound fertility decline.

title: Metabolic comparison of minimally to noninvasive urogenital sample types for studying gynecologic health: A pilot study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study compared metabolic signatures across minimally invasive urogenital sample types—cervicovaginal lavages (CVLs), vaginal swabs, and urine—to identify biomarkers of gynecologic health with implications for understanding vaginal dysbiosis. In healthy women, Lactobacillus species produce lactic acid that acidifies the cervicovaginal environment, but vaginal dysbiosis causes Lactobacillus depletion, overgrowth of dysbiotic anaerobes, and pH elevation. Each sample type revealed distinct metabolic profiles: urine contained the highest diversity of xenobiotics including medications and endocrine-disrupting compounds like parabens and phthalates, CVLs showed enrichment in amino acids and glycerophospholipids, and vaginal swabs displayed predominantly lipid signatures including sphingolipids. Dysbiosis-associated polyamines (putrescine, cadaverine, tyramine, and their acetylated forms) were significantly elevated in non-Lactobacillus dominant women, while acylcarnitine lipids and phospholipids were depleted in dysbiotic samples. Additionally, metabolic signatures related to histidine and urea cycle metabolism were elevated in the overweight/obese subgroup. These findings establish the differential diagnostic utility of urogenital sample types for identifying metabolic dysregulation associated with vaginal dysbiosis and obesity-related metabolic changes.

title: Functional metabolomics: unlocking the role of small molecular metabolites
summary: Functional metabolomics has emerged as a powerful analytical approach for disease diagnosis, biomarker discovery, and drug target identification across multiple disease contexts. The technique enables comprehensive characterization of small molecular metabolites through complementary platforms—GC-MS excels at volatile and TCA cycle metabolites while LC-MS provides superior sensitivity for polar metabolites, lipids, and amino acids—allowing researchers to uncover disease-specific metabolic signatures. Notable applications include the identification of CA19-9 in pancreatic cancer, TMAO precursors in diabetes and cardiovascular disease, and microbial-derived deoxycholic acid in Alzheimer's disease via the AGEs-RAGE inflammatory axis. Metabolomics combined with multi-omics integration has revealed compartment-specific metabolic heterogeneity in tumors, where HIF-1α drives lactate and succinate accumulation characteristic of the Warburg effect. Functional validation studies have also identified therapeutic candidates, exemplified by glibenclamide's suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TGF-β in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease models. Overall, functional metabolomics bridges chemical exposures and metabolic phenotypes, providing critical insights into disease mechanisms and facilitating precision medicine approaches across diverse pathologies.

title: Role of personal care products as endocrine disruptors affecting reproductive age women
summary:
# Summary

Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) found in common personal care products pose significant threats to reproductive health in women by interfering with the hypothalamus-pituitary-gonadal axis and sex steroid hormone signaling. Key culprits include bisphenol A (BPA) from plastics and food containers, phthalates from cosmetics and food packaging, and legacy chemicals like polychlorinated biphenyls and dioxins, which accumulate in human tissues throughout the lifespan. These EDCs operate through multiple mechanisms—mimicking hormones, inhibiting hormone production, and dysregulating gene expression—leading to reduced ovarian reserve, lower antral follicle counts, early puberty, and progression of endometriosis, particularly in advanced and deeply infiltrating forms. Additionally, heavy metals such as lead and mercury disrupt steroidogenic gene expression and estrogen signaling, causing menstrual irregularities and pubertal delays. The evidence collectively demonstrates that exposure to EDCs through modifiable lifestyle factors associated with personal care product use contributes substantially to female infertility and reproductive disorders including polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis.

title: The Power of Strategic Social Media Influencer Communication to Improve Black Women’s Knowledge and Awareness of Environmental Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals: Surveys of Instagram Users
summary:
# Summary

Black women experience disproportionately higher rates of hormone-mediated conditions including uterine fibroids, infertility, and certain cancers, partly due to elevated exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) found in commonly used personal care and hair care products. This study demonstrated that strategic partnerships with social media influencers (SMIs) can effectively increase awareness and behavior change regarding EDC exposures in this population. Through a culturally tailored training program, SMIs substantially improved their own knowledge of specific EDCs like parabens, bisphenol A, and PFAS, and subsequently created content that reached over 16,000 accounts and generated 27,000+ views. Post-intervention, audience members reported significant increases in intentions to avoid specific chemical ingredients, with 80% reporting they would consider company chemical policies and product ingredients when shopping. The results indicate that authentic, culturally relevant SMI communication leveraging environmental justice messaging is a promising strategy to promote health equity and reduce EDC exposures among Black women through improved environmental health literacy and consumer behavior change.

title: Associations Between Endocrine-Disrupting Chemical Exposure and Fertility Outcomes: A Decade of Human Epidemiological Evidence
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive epidemiological review examines how endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—ubiquitous compounds in plastics, food packaging, and cosmetics—impair human fertility through multiple mechanisms. EDCs interfere with endocrine signaling by mimicking estrogen and blocking androgens, thereby disrupting levels of critical reproductive hormones including estradiol, luteinizing hormone, and follicle-stimulating hormone. Specific chemicals demonstrate consistent associations with adverse fertility outcomes: bisphenol A and its analogs correlate with impaired semen quality and polycystic ovary syndrome, phthalates associate with decreased ovarian reserve, and per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances link to infertility. Beyond endometriosis and hormonal dysregulation, EDC exposure compromises both natural and assisted reproductive outcomes by reducing sperm count and motility, impairing gametogenesis, diminishing ovarian reserve, and reducing embryo quality. The evidence demonstrates that EDC-induced tissue and organ developmental impairment extends from altered puberty onset through reduced fecundity and pregnancy maintenance, establishing environmental chemical exposure as a significant public health concern for reproductive health across the lifespan.

title: Occurrence, Migration Behavior, and Environmental Burden of Phthalate Esters in Flooring Materials Used in Newly Renovated Chinese Homes
summary:
# Summary

Phthalate esters (PAEs), particularly DEHP, DBP, and DIBP, are ubiquitous contaminants in flooring materials used in newly renovated Chinese homes, with total concentrations ranging from 1,220 to 166,000 ng/g. Solid wood flooring exhibits significantly higher PAE levels (55,900 ng/g median) compared to engineered and laminate flooring, and demonstrates continuous PAE release, while laminate flooring primarily absorbs PAEs. The dominant factors influencing PAE migration rates are initial PAE concentration, flooring type, and substrate density, with temperature and ventilation further triggering emissions. Chronic exposure to PAEs causes endocrine disruption, reproductive abnormalities, developmental delays, and metabolic disorders, representing a notable health concern through third-hand pollution accumulation on indoor surfaces and dust. The environmental burden of PAEs in homes is significantly influenced by socioeconomic factors including residential floor area, household income, and renovation budget, with eastern and southwestern China exhibiting the highest PAE burdens from flooring materials.

title: Association between novel per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances and premature ovarian insufficiency: a case–control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study demonstrates that novel per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS)—including HFPO-DA, PFBA, PFPeA, and PFPeS—are significantly associated with premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), with higher plasma concentrations detected in POI patients compared to controls. Exposure to these novel PFAS disrupts key reproductive markers by reducing anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count while elevating follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), indicating compromised ovarian reserve. The mechanistic evidence suggests that PFAS compounds disrupt endocrine function and inhibit follicular development, with PFOS activating PPAR-γ signaling to suppress luteinizing hormone and estradiol, while PFOA and PFNA induce granulosa cell apoptosis through mitochondrial disruption. Notably, exposure to PFAS mixtures confers greater POI risk than individual compounds, and the effect is particularly pronounced for HFPO-DA and PFPeA, which show stronger associations with POI risk than legacy PFAS like PFOA and PFOS. These findings highlight emerging environmental chemical exposures as important contributors to ovarian dysfunction and POI development.

title: Association between trace metal element concentrations in human blood plasma and early MAR embryological outcomes: a couple-based prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that plasma trace metal element concentrations significantly influence early embryological outcomes in couples undergoing in vitro fertilization. While certain metals exert harmful effects—particularly cadmium, which impairs ovarian function and reduces blastocyst development, and silver and thallium, which decrease embryo quality and zygote formation—other elements such as iron, lithium, strontium, and molybdenum enhance fertilization rates and blastocyst numbers. In males, lead, zinc, and cadmium adversely affect sperm production, whereas strontium and molybdenum improve embryological outcomes. High exposure to trace metal element mixtures was associated with reduced high-quality embryo and blastocyst counts, while beneficial metal mixtures containing strontium and molybdenum improved early IVF outcomes. The underlying mechanisms likely involve oxidative damage, disruption of reactive oxygen species, and altered gene expression patterns affecting embryonic development, establishing environmental metal exposure as a modifiable factor influencing assisted reproductive success.

title: Associations of Essential and Non-Essential Trace Elements’ Levels in the Blood, Serum, and Urine in Women with Premature Ovarian Insufficiency
summary: This study examined associations between trace element levels and premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) in women, revealing significant metabolic and environmental distinctions between POI patients and controls. Women with POI displayed markedly lower urinary levels of essential trace elements including copper, zinc, selenium, and molybdenum, alongside elevated serum copper, suggesting altered trace element homeostasis that may impair critical reproductive processes. Essential trace elements such as zinc, copper, and selenium are vital for oogenesis, oocyte maturation, and ovulation, while non-essential elements accumulate in ovarian tissue and inhibit folliculogenesis and steroidogenesis through oxidative stress, endocrine disruption, and immune dysregulation. POI patients also exhibited higher body mass index, elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit levels, and greater exposure to environmental pollutants including agricultural and waste-burning proximity, highlighting environmental chemical exposure as a significant determinant of ovarian reserve. The findings underscore how environmental factors, particularly trace element imbalances driven by pollutant exposure, contribute to POI pathogenesis through disruption of essential micronutrient metabolism critical to ovarian function.

title: Endocrine and Reproductive Health Considerations of Sunscreen UV Filters: Insights from a Comprehensive Review 2014–2024
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review (2014–2024) examines how organic ultraviolet filters in sunscreens, particularly benzophenones (BP-1, BP-2, BP-3) and related compounds, act as endocrine disruptors with significant reproductive and developmental consequences. These UV filter chemicals are widely detected in human biological matrices including maternal urine, cord blood, seminal fluid, and breast milk, demonstrating systemic bioaccumulation and environmental persistence. In males, benzophenone exposure reduces testosterone levels, decreases sperm quality and motility, and causes delayed pubertal development and testicular changes, while in females, certain filters accelerate pubertal milestones and are positively associated with endometriosis gene expression. Prenatal exposure to benzophenones is associated with reduced fetal and neonatal weight, decreased birth length, and altered chest circumference, alongside disruption of thyroid and follicle-stimulating hormone levels. The review emphasizes that mixed exposures to multiple UV filters in real-world scenarios produce cumulative endocrine and reproductive health disruptions, raising critical concerns about the safety profile of these ubiquitous cosmetic and personal care product ingredients.

title: Association between ambient air pollution and risk of hand, foot, and mouth disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that ambient air pollution significantly increases the risk of hand, foot, and mouth disease (HFMD), with SO2 showing the strongest association at 51% increased risk, followed by NO2 at 19% and PM10 at 16% elevated risk, while PM2.5, ozone, and carbon monoxide showed no statistically significant associations. HFMD is caused primarily by enterovirus 71, coxsackievirus A16, and coxsackievirus A6, with the latter becoming predominant following widespread EV71 vaccination in China. The mechanistic link between air pollutants and HFMD involves multiple pathways, including particulate matter stimulating respiratory system inflammation, impairing immune function, and potentially carrying pathogenic microorganisms into the respiratory tract. NO2 exposure specifically diminishes virus-specific immune responses, thereby enhancing viral infectivity and disease susceptibility. Additional environmental factors including temperature, humidity, rainfall, and wind speed also contribute to HFMD incidence. These findings establish air pollution as a significant public health risk factor for HFMD transmission and underscore the interconnected relationship between environmental chemical exposures and infectious disease susceptibility.

title: Polyethylene and
summary:
# Summary

This study detected nanoplastics—particularly polyethylene and polyvinyl chloride—at 100% prevalence in both follicular fluid and seminal plasma samples, establishing widespread human reproductive exposure to these environmental contaminants. Nanoplastics induced oxidative stress and triggered reactive oxygen species production, leading to apoptosis, cell cycle arrest, and impaired oocyte maturation in reproductive cells. Higher polyethylene and polyvinyl chloride concentrations were associated with significantly reduced fertilization rates, decreased sperm motility and concentration, increased sperm DNA fragmentation, and lower estradiol levels—indicating compromised ovarian and testicular function. Beyond reproduction, nanoplastics accumulated in multiple organ systems including kidneys, liver, lungs, and spleen, disrupted critical cellular processes such as mitochondrial function, autophagy, and immune responses, and crossed the placental barrier to accumulate in embryonic tissues, causing increased offspring body weight and abnormal brain development in animal models. These findings demonstrate that nanoplastic exposure represents a significant environmental threat to human fertility and potentially transgenerational health outcomes through multiple mechanisms of cellular toxicity.

title: Unraveling the Core of Endometriosis: The Impact of Endocrine Disruptors
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women and is strongly associated with environmental exposure to endocrine-disrupting compounds (EDCs), particularly bisphenol A, phthalates, and dioxins. EDCs promote endometriosis development through multiple interconnected mechanisms: they function as xenoestrogens by binding to estrogen receptors, induce oxidative stress, disrupt progesterone signaling, and trigger epigenetic reprogramming including altered DNA methylation and histone modifications. The xenoestrogen activity of EDCs drives the hallmark pathophysiological features of endometriosis—cellular proliferation, angiogenesis, and immune dysregulation—while simultaneously disrupting the normal anti-inflammatory and differentiating functions of progesterone through progesterone receptor alterations in endometrial lesions. Additionally, EDCs alter the expression of critical enzymes like aromatase and cytochrome P450, further amplifying local estrogen production and establishing a self-perpetuating cycle of tissue damage, chronic inflammation mediated by TNF-α and IL-6, and lesion persistence. These findings underscore endometriosis as a multifactorial disease shaped by environmental chemical exposures acting through epigenetic, hormonal, and immunological pathways.

title: Reproductive Toxicity Effects of Phthalates Based on the Hypothalamic–Pituitary–Gonadal Axis: A Priority Control List Construction from Theoretical Methods
summary:
# Summary

Phthalate esters (PAEs), ubiquitous environmental contaminants identified as endocrine-disrupting chemicals, pose significant reproductive toxicity risks by disrupting the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis at multiple levels. Through molecular docking and dynamics simulations, researchers identified five high-risk PAE molecules—DIPRP, DMEP, DMP, DPP, and DUP—with branched side chain structures conferring greater reproductive toxicity compared to linear counterparts. PAE exposure causes abnormal secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, estrogen, androgens, and gonadotropins, leading to testicular damage, ovarian insufficiency, and increased miscarriage risk in both males and females. In females specifically, PAE exposure adversely affects the ovaries and uterus, promoting endometriosis, infertility, thyroid dysfunction, and pregnancy complications, while in males it impairs sperm quality, morphology, and concentration through mechanisms including DNA damage. Additionally, childhood PAE exposure triggers endocrine disorders including precocious puberty, thyroid disease, and neurodevelopmental effects such as autism and intellectual disabilities, establishing PAEs as priority chemicals requiring environmental control measures.

title: Biodegradable Polymers for Application as Robust Immunomodulatory Biomaterial Carrier Systems
summary: Biodegradable polymers represent a significant advancement over traditional non-degradable biomaterials for immunotherapy delivery and vaccine formulation. While non-degradable carriers like alum have been used clinically for over a century, they cause frustrated phagocytosis, foreign body responses, inflammation, and in rare cases serious adverse events including macrophagic myofasciitis and granuloma formation. Biodegradable polymers such as PLGA, PLA, PGA, PCL, PBAEs, and natural polymers including chitosan, alginate, and hyaluronic acid overcome these limitations by breaking down into monomeric units in vivo while providing tunable degradation rates that enhance antigen presentation and immune cell activation. These polymeric carriers can be engineered to optimize particle size for dendritic cell phagocytosis and lymph node trafficking, and many possess inherent immunomodulatory properties that activate innate immunity and promote cross-presentation to both MHC-I and MHC-II pathways, thereby enhancing cytotoxic T cell education. Additionally, biodegradable polymers eliminate persistent environmental accumulation concerns associated with synthetic non-degradable polymers like PEG, which can persist in the environment and trigger pre-existing immune memory in a large portion of the population.

title: Photodynamic priming overcomes platinum resistance from short-term exposure to select perfluoroalkyl substances in endometrial cancer cell lines
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that select perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), widespread environmental contaminants, induce platinum resistance in endometrial cancer cells by dysregulating mitochondrial function and increasing mitochondrial membrane potential. Photodynamic priming (PDP), a sub-tumoricidal variant of photodynamic therapy, overcomes this chemoresistance when combined with carboplatin in HEC-1B and Ishikawa endometrial cancer cell lines. Two clinically-approved photosensitizers—benzoporphyrin derivative (BPD) and aminolevulinic acid-induced protoporphyrin IX (ALA-PpIX)—were evaluated for their ability to target mitochondrial membranes and restore drug sensitivity. Upon light activation, both photosensitizers induced mitochondrial photodamage and reduced the elevated mitochondrial membrane potential caused by PFAS exposure, with BPD-PDP producing superior mitochondrial membrane potential reduction compared to ALA-PpIX-PDP. These findings establish photodynamic priming as a promising strategy to reverse PFAS-induced platinum resistance in endometrial cancer and underscore the importance of understanding environmental chemical exposure impacts on chemotherapy efficacy.

title: Investigating the causal role of serum metabolites in substance use disorder risk: a study integrating Mendelian randomization and synthesis analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified causal associations between circulating serum metabolites and substance use disorder (SUD) risk, revealing that 77 metabolites have potential causal effects on SUD susceptibility. Key protective metabolites include erythronate, glycerol, and several lipid species (glycerophospholipids and fatty acid derivatives), while risk-elevating metabolites include valine, sphingomyelins, ethyl alpha-glucopyranoside, and gamma-glutamyl-alpha-lysine. The identified metabolites cluster within multiple interconnected pathways affecting amino acid metabolism, energy production (tricarboxylic acid cycle), lipid metabolism, and glutathione homeostasis—mechanisms that converge on neuroinflammation and altered neuroplasticity. Dysregulation of these metabolic pathways, particularly glycerophospholipid and sphingolipid metabolism, may drive chronic inflammation and neurobiological changes that predispose individuals to substance dependence. These findings suggest that specific circulating metabolites could serve as biomarkers for SUD risk stratification and may represent therapeutic targets for prevention and personalized intervention strategies.

title: Association between exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and infections with human papillomavirus and herpes simplex virus type 2
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) is significantly associated with increased prevalence of human papillomavirus (HPV) and herpes simplex virus type 2 (HSV-2) infections. Specific PAH compounds, including 2-naphthalene (2-NAP), phenanthrene (1-PHE), pyrene (1-PYR), and fluorene derivatives (3-FLU, 2-FLU, 9-FLU), showed elevated concentrations in individuals with HPV or HSV-2 infections compared to uninfected controls. The mechanistic basis for these associations involves PAH-induced immunosuppression, as PAHs impair both systemic and mucosal immune responses, thereby reducing host defense against pathogens. Additionally, PAHs possess estrogen-like activity and can cause oocyte DNA damage and ovarian dysfunction, disrupting reproductive health and increasing susceptibility to infections. The associations persisted across age subgroups and smoking status categories, with co-exposure to multiple PAHs demonstrating stronger effects on infection prevalence than individual compounds alone.

title: Bisphenol A migration and associated health risk in polyethylene terephthalate-bottled yogurt drinks (Doogh) under different storage scenarios
summary:
# Summary

Bisphenol A (BPA) migrates from polyethylene terephthalate (PET) bottles into yogurt drinks (doogh) at concentrations that increase significantly with higher storage temperatures, prolonged storage duration, and direct sunlight exposure. As an endocrine-disrupting chemical with estrogen-like properties, BPA is associated with multiple reproductive and systemic health disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, menstrual irregularities, fertility problems, premature puberty, diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease, thyroid dysfunction, and hormone-dependent cancers. The estimated daily intake of BPA from contaminated doogh products is particularly elevated in children due to their lower body weight and higher consumption per kilogram, with exposure levels increasing substantially under adverse storage conditions such as 45°C temperatures or 60 days of storage. These findings highlight the need for improved storage practices and safer packaging alternatives, as the migration of BPA from food contact materials represents a significant source of consumer exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals linked to endometriosis and other reproductive health complications.

title: Xenobiotic Toxicants and Particulate Matter: Effects, Mechanisms, Impacts on Human Health, and Mitigation Strategies
summary:
# Summary

Particulate matter (PM) serves as a significant carrier of xenobiotic toxicants including heavy metals (lead, mercury, cadmium), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, volatile organic compounds, and inorganic salts, with smaller particles possessing greater bioavailability and systemic reach. These environmental pollutants induce harmful biological responses through oxidative stress and systemic inflammation, mechanisms amplified when transition metals like iron and copper catalyze reactive oxygen species formation via Fenton reactions. The xenobiotic toxicants themselves cause immunotoxicity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and genotoxicity, while PM exposure disrupts endocrine function and reproductive health, leading to adverse outcomes including birth defects and low birth weight. Ultra-fine particles can translocate into the bloodstream and disseminate to vital organs including the heart, brain, and kidneys, triggering macrophage responses that result in granuloma formation in lungs and lymph nodes. Beyond respiratory disease, PM exposure is associated with cardiovascular diseases, hypertension, neurological and neurodegenerative diseases, and cognitive decline. These findings underscore the multisystem toxicity of environmental particulate contamination and suggest that endometriosis may represent an additional reproductive health manifestation of xenobiotic and oxidative stress-mediated pathology.

title: The Impact of Endocrine Disruptors on the Female Genital Tract Microbiome: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Endocrine disruptors (EDs) such as bisphenol A, phthalates, parabens, pesticides, dioxins, and polychlorinated biphenyls disrupt the female genital tract microbiome through multiple mechanisms, causing hormone perturbations, immune modulation, and epithelial barrier breach. These xenobiotics bind to and mimic estrogen and androgen receptors, disrupt steroidogenesis, affect epigenetic modifications including DNA methylation and histone acetylation, and modulate cytokine production and immune signaling, collectively leading to microbial dysbiosis. A healthy vaginal microbiome dominated by Lactobacillus species—which produce lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins—maintains vaginal acidification and supports a bidirectional estrogen-microbiota relationship through β-glucuronidase-mediated estrogen metabolism; ED exposure disrupts this balance by reducing glycogen supply and epithelial proliferation, eliminating conditions necessary for Lactobacillus dominance. Dysbiosis triggered by ED exposure is strongly associated with bacterial vaginosis, infertility, preterm birth, urogenital infections, and pelvic inflammatory disease, underscoring the critical role of environmental chemical exposures in reproductive health complications. The evidence indicates that even low-dose ED exposure can trigger adverse effects on the female reproductive tract through microbiome disruption, highlighting the need for strategies to minimize exposure to ubiquitous endocrine-disrupting chemicals in consumer products and pesticides.

title: Effect of Nitrous Oxide During Oocyte Retrieval on In Vitro Fertilization Outcomes: A Retrospective Study
summary: This retrospective study examined how nitrous oxide (N₂O) exposure during oocyte retrieval affects in vitro fertilization outcomes. Nitrous oxide irreversibly inhibits methionine synthetase, an enzyme essential for DNA synthesis and methylation reactions, thereby reducing thymidine availability and potentially damaging dividing cells such as oocytes. The study found that N₂O exposure was associated with a significantly increased number of Grade 3 (lower-quality) oocytes compared to control groups, suggesting acute toxic effects on developing gametes. However, despite this reduction in oocyte quality, N₂O exposure did not negatively affect fertilization rates, cleavage rates, or clinical pregnancy rates, indicating that short-term anesthetic exposure during the retrieval procedure does not substantially compromise downstream reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that while N₂O may impair oocyte morphology through enzyme inhibition, the brief exposure during retrieval does not translate into clinically significant reductions in fertility, though longer-term implications for diminished ovarian reserve remain a consideration.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of ischemic heart disease attributable to lead exposure, 1990–2021: decomposition, frontier, and projection analysis
summary: Lead exposure is a significant environmental risk factor that causes ischemic heart disease and multiple systemic health effects through cardiotoxic mechanisms. Humans encounter lead through food, water, and inhalation, with chronic exposure leading to accumulation in tissues including bone, blood, hair, and teeth, subsequently damaging neurological, hematological, cardiovascular, and renal systems. Lead induces cardiopathology via effects on cardiac function, humoral and neuronal dysregulation, and hypertension, ultimately increasing risk of myocardial infarction and subclinical myocardial injury. Globally in 2021, lead exposure caused approximately 590,370 deaths and 11.9 million disability-adjusted life years from ischemic heart disease, with higher burden in males, older adults, South Asia, and North Africa/Middle East regions, driven primarily by population growth and aging. Males face elevated occupational exposure through telecommunications, plumbing, and construction work, while estrogen may provide cardioprotection in females. Beyond ischemic heart disease, lead exposure is associated with stroke, hypertensive heart disease, diabetes, and kidney disease, making it a critical public health concern amenable to prevention through elimination of lead paint and reduction of environmental contamination.

title: Talc, body powder, and ovarian cancer: A summary of the epidemiologic evidence
summary:
# Summary: Talc, Body Powder, and Ovarian Cancer

Multiple epidemiological studies have demonstrated a significant association between genital talc and body powder use and ovarian cancer risk, with talc particles detected in ovarian cancer tissue samples and throughout the female reproductive tract in exposed individuals. Talc's carcinogenic potential is partly attributable to historical asbestos contamination and the presence of other Class 1 carcinogens like quartz, though the mineral itself was classified as carcinogenic by IARC in 2010. The proposed mechanisms involve talc particles triggering inflammatory responses in peritoneal and pleural tissues, stimulating release of cytokines and chemokines that may promote tumor development. Genetic variations in glutathione S-transferase genes (GSTM1 and GSTT1) appear to modulate individual susceptibility to talc-associated ovarian cancer risk, and estrogen exposure may partially explain the observed associations. While systematic reviews and meta-analyses of case-control data report elevated ovarian cancer risk with powder use, the magnitude and clinical significance of this association remains subject to ongoing debate in the literature.

title: Pregnancy urinary phenol biomarker concentrations in relation to serum levels of inflammatory cytokines: Results from the EARTH study
summary:
# Summary

The EARTH study demonstrates that prenatal exposure to environmental phenols triggers inflammatory disturbances through multiple interconnected pathways during pregnancy, a period of heightened physiological vulnerability. Phenols enhance production of key pro-inflammatory cytokines—IL-1β, TNFα, and IL-6—primarily by inducing oxidative stress that activates the TLR4/NF-κB pathway, MAPK cascade, and NLRP3 inflammasome, while simultaneously disrupting Nrf2-mediated antioxidant defenses. Specific phenolic compounds show trimester-dependent associations with inflammatory markers; for example, ethylparaben and butylparaben correlate with elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP), whereas parabens' estrogen-like activity and interactions with hormone receptors may dysregulate the maternal immune system and impair maternal-fetal tolerance. The study reveals that pregnancy involves distinct immune transitions—pro-inflammatory in the first and third trimesters but anti-inflammatory in the second—providing critical windows where phenol exposure could disrupt normal immunological adaptation and increase risks for adverse outcomes including preterm birth and gestational complications. These findings underscore how endocrine-disrupting phenols compromise the delicate balance of pregnancy-related inflammation necessary for both fetal development and labor initiation.

title: Environmentally relevant concentrations of individual per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and a PFAS mixture impact proliferation, migration, and gene transcription in a human myometrial cell line
summary: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) at environmentally relevant concentrations adversely affect human myometrial cell function through multiple mechanisms relevant to reproductive health. Individual PFAS compounds—including PFOA, PFOS, GENX, and PFBS—and PFAS mixtures significantly enhanced cell proliferation, viability, and migration in human uterine cells, with PFOA notably driving cell cycle progression from G0/G1 to G2/M phase. These exposures substantially disrupted gap junction intercellular communication, particularly via PFOA, PFBS, and PFAS mixtures, a mechanism that could impair normal uterine cell coordination and function. At the transcriptional level, different PFAS compounds altered expression of 89–150 genes each, with shared enrichment in pathways governing cell-to-cell signaling, lipid metabolism, and cell death/survival, alongside consistent negative enrichment of the Gap Junction pathway. These findings provide mechanistic support for epidemiological evidence linking PFAS exposure to increased risk of reproductive diseases including endometriosis and uterine fibroids.

title: Research trends and hotspots of infertility and phthalate esters: a bibliometric and visualization analysis from 2001 to 2024
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis identifies phthalate esters (PAEs) as major environmental contaminants strongly associated with infertility and reproductive dysfunction. PAEs function as endocrine-disrupting chemicals that impair the hypothalamus-pituitary-gonadal axis, leading to abnormal gonadal hormone secretion and alterations in sex hormone receptor synthesis, ultimately causing testicular damage and reduced fertility capacity. Beyond reproductive effects, PAEs induce oxidative stress and apoptosis while contributing to metabolic and psychological complications including obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and pregnancy-related anxiety and depression. The analysis reveals that research hotspots center on endocrine disruption mechanisms, oxidative stress pathways, and hormone interactions, with emerging focus on in-utero and prenatal exposures as critical windows of vulnerability for reproductive health impairment. Bisphenol A and other endocrine-disrupting chemicals show similar associations with infertility, suggesting a broader class effect of environmental chemicals on reproductive outcomes in industrialized populations.

title: Polycystic ovary syndrome and organochlorine pesticides: exploring potential links and mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) and related persistent organic pollutants represent a significant environmental risk factor for polycystic ovary syndrome through their action as endocrine-disrupting chemicals that bioaccumulate in living organisms. These compounds disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis by binding estrogen receptors and modulating steroidogenic enzymes, leading to aberrant gonadotropin-releasing hormone signaling, excessive luteinizing hormone secretion, elevated androgen production, and the characteristic hormonal imbalances of PCOS. Additionally, OCPs promote insulin resistance and obesity, which synergistically exacerbate hyperandrogenism through increased insulin-stimulated ovarian steroidogenesis and reduced sex hormone-binding globulin synthesis. Human biomonitoring studies demonstrate elevated serum OCP levels in PCOS patients compared to controls, while specific pesticide compounds such as methoxychlor, dieldrin, and endosulfan directly alter hypothalamic gene expression and pituitary function in animal models. Beyond OCPs, emerging evidence implicates bisphenol A, polychlorinated biphenyls, phthalates, and per/polyfluoroalkyl substances as contributors to PCOS pathogenesis through similar endocrine-disrupting mechanisms, particularly in individuals with elevated body mass index.

title: A Prospective Study of Hair Dyes and Uterine Leiomyomata Incidence in the Study of Environment, Lifestyle, and Fibroids
summary: This prospective study examines the association between hair dye use and uterine fibroids (leiomyomata), revealing that hair dye products—particularly non-permanent rinses that fade—are modestly associated with increased fibroid risk in women. Hair dyes contain multiple endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) including aromatic amines, phthalates, phenols, metals, and formaldehyde, which alter circulating hormone levels such as estradiol and disrupt menstrual cycling. Black women face disproportionate exposure risk due to earlier and more frequent use of hair products from childhood, greater use of dark-shade dyes, and scalp damage from relaxers that enhances EDC absorption.
