The cumulative hormonal effects of these exposures appear particularly relevant given that fibroids are ovarian hormone-dependent lesions that cause significant morbidity including heavy menstrual bleeding, pain, infertility, and anemia. Notably, adolescent exposure to hair dyes may confer particular vulnerability given prior associations with hormone-dependent cancers and early menarche. These findings underscore how occupational and consumer chemical exposures, combined with patterns of use in Black communities, contribute to observed racial disparities in fibroid incidence and severity.

title: A Systematic Review of Estrogens as Emerging Contaminants in Water: A Global Overview Study from the One Health Perspective
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines estrogens as environmental contaminants from a One Health perspective, identifying them as a significant threat to environmental integrity, wildlife health, and human well-being. Estrone, estradiol, ethinylestradiol, and estriol were the most frequently detected estrogens across diverse water sources, including wastewater treatment plant effluents, rivers, lakes, surface waters, and drinking water supplies, with concentrations ranging from nanograms to micrograms per liter. These environmental estrogens function as endocrine-disrupting chemicals that cause homeostasis disruption, hormonal imbalance, and ecosystem damage, with documented effects including cancer, gestational complications, congenital malformations, obesity, and impaired spermatogenesis in humans. In aquatic organisms, estrogen exposure induces population-level changes in fish including altered gender ratios, impaired egg production, cytogenotoxicity, and hepatocarcinogenic processes, demonstrating widespread ecological consequences. The sources of contamination include direct release from wastewater treatment plants and indirect pathways from industrial, urban, and agricultural effluents. These findings underscore the urgent need for comprehensive strategies to mitigate estrogen contamination and protect both human health and ecosystem integrity globally.

title: Dynamic Changes of Selected Signaling Molecules in Ovaries Following Early-Life Exposure to Fumonisin B1 in Wistar Rats in Association With DNA Methylation
summary: Early-life exposure to fumonisin B1, a mycotoxin present in the food chain, causes ovarian damage through dual mechanisms involving epigenetic modifications and signaling pathway disruption in Wistar rats. The toxin upregulates the DNA methyltransferase Dnmt3b in a dose-dependent manner, leading to DNA hypermethylation that alters gene expression and contributes to ovarian dysfunction across generations. Fumonisin B1 simultaneously dysregulates the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling pathway critical for follicle activation and granulosa cell proliferation, causing high-dose exposure to decrease mTOR expression while paradoxically increasing Akt in the second generation, disrupting normal ovarian development. Additionally, high-dose exposure downregulates key ovarian genes including Cyp19 and Esr2, which are essential for steroidogenesis and ovarian function, resulting in decreased primordial follicles and accelerated reproductive aging. These epigenetic and signaling alterations are associated with polycystic ovary syndrome-like pathology and have intergenerational effects, demonstrating that early environmental toxin exposure can program adult-onset ovarian disease through heritable epigenetic changes. The findings highlight how endocrine-disrupting chemicals compromise reproductive health through interconnected mechanisms involving DNA methylation and critical developmental signaling pathways.

title: Endocrine disruption to metastasis: How phthalates promote breast carcinogenesis
summary:
# Summary

Phthalates, ubiquitous endocrine-disrupting chemicals found in plastics, cosmetics, and food packaging, promote breast carcinogenesis through multiple mechanisms centered on estrogen receptor signaling activation. While hereditary factors account for only 5–10% of breast cancer incidence, environmental exposures like phthalates drive disease initiation, progression, and metastasis in the endocrine-responsive breast tissue. Mechanistically, phthalates activate estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) signaling and downstream pathways including PI3K/AKT/mTOR and aryl hydrocarbon receptor signaling, which promote oncogenic gene expression (MYC, BCL2, cyclin D1) and enhance cell proliferation while suppressing apoptosis. Additionally, phthalates including DEHP and BBP induce therapy resistance by antagonizing chemotherapeutic agents like paclitaxel and doxorubicin through CYP enzyme induction and by inhibiting tamoxifen-induced apoptosis. Human exposure occurs across critical developmental windows including intrauterine periods through ingestion, inhalation, and dermal contact, with prenatal exposure linked to adverse fetal outcomes. These findings underscore how widespread phthalate exposure acts as a significant nongenetic determinant of breast cancer risk through endocrine disruption and treatment resistance mechanisms.

title: A Systematic Literature Review of Reproductive Toxicological Studies on Phthalates
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that phthalate exposure, particularly di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) and dibutyl phthalate (DBP), poses significant reproductive toxicological risks through multiple interconnected mechanisms. In females, phthalates reduce estradiol levels and ovarian weight while increasing oxidative stress and follicular degeneration, effects mediated partly through activation of estrogen receptors and disruption of antioxidant signaling pathways like Nrf2/HO-1. In males, DEHP exposure decreases sperm count and testosterone levels while compromising the blood-testis barrier through downregulation of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, connexin-43, N-cadherin), and phthalate mixtures disrupt spermatogenesis by downregulating essential genes including PIWIL1 and PIWIL2. The metabolite MEHP triggers reproductive cell death through multiple pathways including apoptosis, autophagy, and inflammatory responses via elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) and oxidative stress. Mechanistically, phthalates activate NF-κB signaling through zinc transporter upregulation, promoting pyroptosis in ovarian granulosa cells, while also upregulating steroidogenic enzymes despite downregulating StAR protein. These findings collectively establish phthalates as endocrine-disrupting chemicals with documented presence in human biological samples that substantially compromise both male and female reproductive function.

title: Airborne PM10 Decreases Ku80 Expression and Ku70–Ku80 Heterodimer Levels of the Non-Homologous End Joining Repair Pathway in Lung Epithelial Cells
summary: Airborne particulate matter with a diameter of 10 micrometers or less (PM10) induces double-strand breaks in lung epithelial cells and simultaneously impairs the non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway, the primary DNA repair mechanism responsible for fixing approximately 80% of double-strand breaks. PM10 exposure downregulates critical NHEJ components, specifically reducing expression and protein levels of Ku80 and XRCC4, and decreasing the formation of the Ku70–Ku80 heterodimer complex essential for initiating repair. This disruption is particularly concerning because PM10-exposed cells demonstrate persistent genomic instability, characterized by elevated gamma-H2AX foci and increased micronuclei formation, contrasting with doxorubicin-treated cells that successfully resolve DNA damage markers within 24 hours. The accumulation of unrepaired DNA damage in PM10-exposed cells suggests that chronic environmental exposure impairs genomic stability through mechanisms potentially involving oxidative stress, which could contribute to the carcinogenic effects of air pollution exposure linked to lung cancer development.

title: Environmental Insults to Glucose Metabolism: The Role of Pollutants in Insulin Resistance
summary:
# Summary

Insulin regulates glucose metabolism through binding to insulin receptors on β-cells, which triggers autophosphorylation and downstream signaling via PI3K and protein kinase B to facilitate GLUT4-dependent glucose transport. Insulin resistance—characterized by impaired glucose uptake and increased gluconeogenesis via FoxO1—is associated with type 2 diabetes, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, and polycystic ovarian syndrome, with risk factors including obesity, adipokine dysfunction, and dysregulation by hormones such as cortisol and glucagon. Environmental chemical exposures, particularly endocrine-disrupting chemicals like arsenic, persistent organic pollutants, phthalates, bisphenol, phenols, and parabens, promote insulin resistance and have been linked to endometriosis development. Particulate matter exposure, especially PM2.5, drives insulin resistance through systemic inflammation and oxidative stress while elevating glucocorticoids, ACTH, and CRH, and disrupts gut microbiota and sphingolipid metabolism. Nitrogen dioxide and sulfur dioxide from air pollution induce reactive oxygen species generation and activate NF-κB and MAPK pathways, increasing pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 that impair insulin signaling and cause endothelial dysfunction. These findings underscore how modern environmental pollutants compromise glucose homeostasis through interconnected inflammatory, oxidative, and metabolic pathways, with implications for metabolic disease and endometriosis risk.

title: The Presence of Micro- and Nanoplastics in Food and the Estimation of the Amount Consumed Depending on Dietary Patterns
summary:
# Summary

Micro- and nanoplastics (MNPs) have become ubiquitous environmental contaminants found across multiple exposure routes, with ingestion identified as the primary pathway for human exposure. MNPs contaminate diverse food sources including fruits, vegetables, seafood, drinking water, and processed foods, with dietary patterns significantly influencing cumulative intake—lacto-ovo-vegetarian diets result in the highest MNP consumption at approximately 69.1 × 10^6 particles daily, while Mediterranean diets provide more balanced exposure at 37.5 × 10^6 particles daily. Beyond food, MNPs originate from both primary sources such as intentionally added plastic microbeads in cosmetics and toothpaste, and secondary sources including plastic degradation, synthetic textile abrasion, and vehicle tire wear. Once ingested or inhaled, MNPs can penetrate multiple physiological barriers; particles measuring 0.1–10 μm reach pulmonary alveoli and may migrate systemically, with the smallest nanoplastics capable of crossing blood-brain and placental barriers. MNPs exert harmful effects on human health by disrupting reproduction, endocrine function, and immune system activity, mirroring toxicological impacts on morphology, physiology, biochemistry, and genetics across plant and animal organisms.

title: Effects of Gossypol Exposure on Ovarian Reserve Function: Comprehensive Risk Assessment Based on TRAEC Strategy
summary: Gossypol, a naturally occurring polyphenolic compound found in cottonseed oil and cottonseed meal, acts as a moderate reproductive toxicant that severely compromises ovarian reserve function through multiple mechanisms. Exposure to gossypol causes dose-dependent depletion of primordial, primary, secondary, and antral follicles while simultaneously increasing follicular atresia and oocyte degeneration, ultimately impairing folliculogenesis and reducing viable follicle counts. At the cellular level, gossypol inhibits oocyte maturation by suppressing germinal vesicle breakdown and polar body extrusion, induces spindle abnormalities and chromosomal misalignment, reduces mitochondrial activity, and triggers apoptosis in granulosa cells through inhibition of oncogenic proteins APE1 and BCL-2 family members. Proteomic analysis reveals that gossypol's toxicity involves disruption of steroidogenesis, immune regulation, and mitochondrial metabolism, resulting in disrupted estrous cycles, impaired embryogenesis, and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Importantly, gossypol accumulates in the liver, kidneys, bile, and spleen, exerting hepatotoxic and neurotoxic effects, and damage to ovarian reserve and oocyte competence shows only partial reversibility even after withdrawal. Despite its clinical use in managing endometriosis and uterine fibroids, the high reproductive toxicity risk poses significant concerns for populations exposed through dietary consumption of cottonseed products.

title: Difference in association between dioxin-like compounds and all-cause mortality among non-menopause and menopausal women: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that dioxin-like compounds (DLCs), including specific polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) and dioxins, are associated with increased all-cause mortality in women, with differential effects based on menopausal status. DLCs exert their effects by binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor and inducing anti-estrogenic effects, disrupting the androgen/estrogen balance, and directly affecting the release of follicle-stimulating and luteinizing hormones. The study identified seven specific compounds—PCB74, PCB105, PCB118, PCB156, HXCDD, OCDD, and PNCDF—as significantly associated with mortality risk, though with distinct exposure-response patterns: PCB105, PCB118, PCB156, and OCDD increased mortality risk even at low concentrations, while PCB66, PCB74, HXCDD, and PNCDF required moderate to high concentrations. Notably, mortality associations differed by menopausal status, with PCB156 and HXCDD showing stronger associations in non-menopausal women, while PCB105, PCB118, and OCDD were more prominently associated in naturally menopausal women. The findings underscore that women, particularly those who have undergone menopause, are more susceptible to DLC exposure effects.

title: Design and prioritization of the French surveillance program of health effects in the context of exposure to endocrine disruptors using a Delphi study
summary:
# Summary

This French public health surveillance study employed a Delphi consensus methodology to prioritize health effects associated with endocrine disruptor (ED) exposure. Accumulating evidence demonstrates that endocrine disruptors cause or significantly contribute to multiple disease categories including neurodevelopmental disorders, metabolic diseases, reproductive dysfunction, immune dysregulation, thyroid disorders, and hormone-dependent cancers such as breast and prostate cancer. Biomonitoring data reveal widespread human exposure to multiple endocrine disruptors including phthalates, bisphenols, pesticides, and polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) in the French population. Among the prioritized health outcomes, endometriosis emerged as particularly relevant to endocrine disruptor exposure, alongside other reproductive effects such as altered sperm quality and neurodevelopmental conditions including autism spectrum disorder and attention deficit disorder. This comprehensive surveillance framework establishes endocrine disruptors as significant public health threats affecting multiple organ systems across the lifespan.

title: Long-term exposure to ambient air pollutants and female infertility risk: a population-based cohort study in Taiwan
summary: This population-based cohort study in Taiwan demonstrates that long-term exposure to multiple ambient air pollutants significantly increases female infertility risk in reproductive-aged women. Particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and nitrogen oxides (NOX, NO, NO2) all showed consistent dose-dependent associations with elevated infertility hazard, with PM2.5 demonstrating particularly strong effects (hazard ratio 1.74). The mechanisms underlying these associations appear to involve direct ovarian toxicity, as PM2.5 exposure correlates with reduced ovarian follicle counts, altered estrous cyclicity, implantation failures, and decreased anti-müllerian hormone levels. Notably, ozone (O3) exposure showed an inverse protective relationship, reducing infertility risk by 52%. The study also identified endometriosis as a comorbidity in 3.80% of the cohort, linking environmental chemical exposures to this reproductive pathology. These findings underscore environmental air quality as a modifiable risk factor for female infertility and suggest that pollution reduction strategies may have reproductive health benefits.

title: Serum phthalates and risks of incident cardiovascular disease among individuals with type 2 diabetes in a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study in individuals with type 2 diabetes reveals a selective association between serum phthalate exposure and stroke risk, independent of broader cardiovascular disease. Multiple phthalate compounds—including di-n-butyl phthalate (DnBP), low-molecular-weight phthalates (ΣLMW), dimethyl phthalate (DMP), and diethyl phthalate (DEP)—demonstrated dose-dependent increases in incident stroke risk, with hazard ratios ranging from 1.36 to 1.53 in highest exposure quartiles. Notably, no significant associations were observed between phthalate exposure and incident coronary heart disease or general cardiovascular disease risk in this population. Mechanistically, phthalates function as endocrine and metabolic disruptors that promote cardiovascular injury through multiple pathways: DEHP (di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate) induces oxidative stress, inflammation, and calcium homeostasis disruption while activating matrix metalloproteinases that drive vascular smooth muscle proliferation. Subgroup analyses identified smoking status and elevated BMI as modifiers of phthalate-associated stroke risk, suggesting environmental chemical exposures may selectively increase cerebrovascular but not coronary disease risk in diabetic populations.

title: The Endocrine-Disrupting Chemical Benzophenone-3 in Concentrations Ranging from 0.001 to 10 µM Does Not Affect the Human Decidualization Process in an In Vitro Setting
summary:
# Summary

Benzophenone-3 (BP-3), a widely used endocrine-disrupting chemical UV filter found in human blood, urine, amniotic fluid, and breast milk, was investigated for its effects on human decidualization—the transformation of endometrial stromal cells essential for embryo implantation. Despite concerns that endocrine disruptors can impair reproductive processes and lead to implantation failure and miscarriage, this in vitro study found that BP-3 exposure at concentrations ranging from 0.001 to 10 µM did not significantly affect decidualization markers including prolactin, IGFBP1, and NCOA3, nor did it alter stromal cell viability, proliferation, or expression of matrix metalloproteinases involved in trophoblastic invasion. Trophoblastic spheroid attachment and outgrowth into endometrial stromal cells were also unaffected by any tested BP-3 concentration. These findings contrast with other studies showing that BP-3 can induce apoptosis and cell-cycle arrest in endometrial cells, suggesting that the effects of endocrine disruptors may be cell-type and context-dependent, and highlighting the complexity of non-monotonic dose-response relationships observed with certain chemicals.

title: The effect of phytoestrogens and PAHs on endometriosis and the involvement of gut microbiota, inflammation, and molecular targets
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, is influenced by complex interactions between environmental chemical exposures, gut microbiota metabolites, and molecular pathways. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and phthalates—endocrine disrupting chemicals present in air, food, and consumer products—increase endometriosis risk through mechanisms involving oxidative stress, immune modulation, hormonal disruption, and inflammatory responses. Conversely, enterolactone, a gut microbiota-derived metabolite of dietary phytoestrogens, is significantly lower in endometriosis patients and protects against disease by binding to estrogen receptor ESR1 and the enzyme XDH, thereby reducing uric acid production, a key inflammatory mediator. The study identifies specific PAH metabolites (1-OHPHE, 1-OHNAP, 2-OHNAP, and fluorene metabolites) as positively associated with endometriosis risk, while enterolactone and certain phytoestrogens exhibit protective effects potentially mediated through the PI3K-Akt pathway and lipid metabolism alterations. These findings underscore how environmental exposures disrupt the axis between dietary compounds, gut microbiota function, and systemic inflammation to promote endometriosis development, suggesting that enhancing enterolactone production through dietary intervention may mitigate PAH-induced disease risk.

title: Pesticides, an urgent challenge to global environmental health and planetary boundaries
summary:
# Summary

Pesticides pose a multifaceted threat to human health and environmental integrity through multiple mechanisms of toxicity. These chemicals act as endocrine disruptors that bind to hormone receptors, causing developmental and reproductive toxicity, while simultaneously disrupting critical planetary boundaries including biogeochemical cycles and biosphere integrity. Specific pesticides demonstrate distinct harmful pathways: DDT binds to estrogen receptors and induces oxidative stress, leading to cancer, immune dysfunction, and reproductive health disruption; malathion and diazinon damage testicular Leydig cells and reduce androgen production; and neonicotinoids bind nicotinic acetylcholine receptors causing neuronal excitability and paralysis. Occupational and prenatal pesticide exposures are associated with male reproductive toxicity and spontaneous abortion, while various pesticides including paraquat and chlorpyrifos cause neurotoxicity. Despite providing agricultural productivity benefits and disease vector control, the expanding global pesticide use from 1.81 to 3.69 million metric tons between 1990 and the present correlates with increasing negative health outcomes, creating an urgent challenge requiring comprehensive regulatory intervention.

title: QuEChERS analytical approach for monitoring endocrine-disrupting chemicals in human urine
summary: This study employs a QuEChERS-based analytical method to detect and quantify endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) in human urine, focusing on three major chemical classes: organophosphate esters (OPEs), phthalates, and parabens. These ubiquitous environmental contaminants—found in flame retardants, plastics, food packaging, cosmetics, and personal care products—are known to disrupt the endocrine system by altering progesterone and testosterone levels, with suspected mutagenic, carcinogenic, and neurotoxic effects. The analysis revealed that all three EDC classes were detected in human urine samples, with parabens being the most abundant (detected in 100% of samples), followed by phthalate metabolites, and OPE metabolites at lower concentrations. Notable findings included MEP as the most prevalent phthalate metabolite and 4-HB and 3,4-DHB as ubiquitous paraben metabolites, while age emerged as a significant factor with teenagers showing significantly higher levels of OPEs and parabens compared to adults. These results underscore widespread human exposure to multiple EDCs simultaneously and highlight the need for continued monitoring of these chemicals given their associations with reproductive and metabolic health issues.

title: Association between DDT or its byproducts and T2DM: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a significant positive association between DDT exposure and its metabolites with type 2 diabetes mellitus risk, with a combined odds ratio of 1.13 (95% CI: 1.08–1.15). The primary DDT metabolite p,p′-DDE showed the strongest association with T2DM, particularly in biomarker-stratified analyses. DDT and related organochlorine pesticides induce insulin resistance through multiple molecular mechanisms, including inhibition of key insulin signaling regulators (Insig-1, Lpin1, and insulin receptor substrate 2), suppression of pancreatic β-cell protein expression, and activation of endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways involving PERK and IRE1a upregulation. Additionally, DDT exposure triggers inflammation and oxidative stress that further impair glucose homeostasis and insulin signaling through the PI3K/Akt pathway and GLUT4 translocation. These persistent organic pollutants accumulate in metabolically active tissues including adipose tissue, liver, pancreas, and kidneys, establishing a mechanistic link between environmental chemical exposure and metabolic disease development.

title: Ultrasensitive
summary: This study establishes hair as an ultrasensitive biomonitor for detecting environmental chemical exposures relevant to endometriosis research. Hair effectively accumulates and retains multiple classes of environmental contaminants including phthalates (dibutyl, diethyl, diethylhexyl, and benzyl butyl phthalate), trace elements, hormones, and drugs through interactions with the circulatory system at the hair follicle. Multiple phthalate metabolites were identified in human hair samples, with dibutyl phthalate and its metabolite monobutyl phthalate being particularly notable as known endocrine disruptors commonly found as environmental plasticizers. Beyond phthalates, the analysis revealed exposure markers from diverse environmental sources including cigarette smoke components (crotonaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acrolein), personal care products (siloxanes, oxybenzone), vehicle exhaust (methylfuran), and building materials/paints (isophorone). This comprehensive hair-based biomonitoring approach offers a non-invasive method to assess cumulative environmental chemical burdens, particularly endocrine-disrupting compounds that may contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Evaluation of the effects of exposure to heavy metals in rescue dogs and their role as environmental biomonitors after the Brumadinho disaster, Minas Gerais, Brazil
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated heavy metal exposure in rescue dogs deployed after the Brumadinho mining disaster and identified them as potential environmental biomonitors of contamination. Rescue dogs exposed to the disaster site accumulated significantly elevated cadmium levels, which negatively correlated with IL-15, a key anti-inflammatory cytokine, while cadmium simultaneously activated multiple pro-inflammatory mediators including NF-κB, IL-6, and TNF-α. Exposed dogs exhibited marked clinical pathology including dermatological and locomotor system signs, with 30% developing neoplasms in skin and reproductive tissues. The study highlights cadmium's multi-organ toxicity pathway involving suppression of protective immunity and sustained inflammatory activation, mechanisms that extend to documented reproductive effects including endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and pregnancy loss in humans. These findings underscore heavy metal contamination as a significant environmental health hazard linked to both acute clinical disease and chronic reproductive dysfunction, with rescue dogs serving as biological sentinels for ecosystem and human health risks.

title: Microplastic exposure induces epithelial barrier alterations and increases collagen deposition in a 3D human endometrial model in vitro
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that microplastic exposure damages the human endometrial barrier and promotes fibrotic remodeling using a three-dimensional endometrial model. At higher concentrations (10–50 mg/ml) over 48 hours, polystyrene microplastics compromised epithelial integrity by reducing transepithelial electrical resistance and downregulating tight junction proteins ZO1 and CDH1, while simultaneously penetrating into the stromal compartment. Microplastic exposure activated pro-fibrotic signaling pathways—including upregulation of transforming growth factor-beta (TGFβ), Wnt signaling, and p53—leading to increased expression of collagen genes (COL1A2, COL3A1, COL4A2) and decreased matrix metalloproteinase expression (MMP1, MMP2, MMP9), resulting in net collagen accumulation. These alterations—epithelial barrier dysfunction coupled with excessive collagen deposition—phenotypically resemble endometriosis-related pathologies. The findings indicate that environmental microplastic exposure represents a previously underrecognized hazard to endometrial health, potentially contributing to the pathogenesis of reproductive disorders including endometriosis.

title: Effects of Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals in the Brain: The Example of Neurodevelopment Alterations upon Exposure In Utero to Synthetic Sex Hormones
summary: In utero exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—including pesticides, bisphenols, phthalates, and polychlorinated biphenyls—alters multiple neurodevelopmental pathways and increases risk for psychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorders. EDCs exert their effects by binding to cell membrane and nuclear receptors, disrupting sex steroid signaling and thyroid hormone function, both of which are essential for critical neurodevelopmental processes including neurogenesis, neuronal migration, myelination, and synaptic plasticity. Prenatal exposure to synthetic sex hormones such as diethylstilbestrol and synthetic progestins is associated with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder, and other mood disorders, reflecting their roles in regulating neurotransmitter systems including the dopaminergic, glutamatergic, and GABAergic pathways. Beyond neuropsychiatric effects, EDCs are linked to intellectual disability, autism spectrum disorders, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, and neurodegenerative diseases, as well as reproductive abnormalities including endometriosis, cryptorchidism, hypospadias, early puberty, and testicular cancer. These findings demonstrate that EDC exposure during critical developmental windows disrupts both the endocrine and nervous systems through convergent mechanisms, with broad implications for public health policy and chemical safety assessment.

title: The Silent Conquest: The Journey of Micro- and Nanoplastics Through Children’s Organs
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the widespread exposure of children to micro- and nanoplastics (MNPs) through environmental sources and investigates the resulting biological consequences. MNPs are ubiquitously present in marine waters, soils, atmosphere, tap water, and indoor environments, with documented accumulation in human tissues including blood, the placental barrier, and the central nervous system. Children face particular vulnerability due to immature detoxification systems and critical developmental windows, experiencing multiple adverse effects from MNP exposure including endocrine disruption, immune dysregulation, and neurodevelopmental impairment. Specific exposure pathways include ingestion of contaminated infant formula containing polyethylene, polypropylene, and polyethylene terephthalate; inhalation of particles from indoor sources such as tatami mats and carpets; and consumption of food from plastic-contaminated sources, with microplastics detected in pediatric lungs and stool samples. Chemical additives leached from plastics, particularly phthalates and bisphenols, cause thyroid dysfunction by altering TSH and free T4 levels, while ingested microplastics disrupt the gut microbiota by reducing alpha diversity and beneficial bacterial genera such as Alistipes and Parabacteroides. These mechanisms collectively suggest that pediatric MNP exposure represents a significant emerging health threat operating through multiple organ systems and biological pathways.

title: Prenatal Dietary Exposure to Endocrine Disruptors and Its Lasting Impact on Offspring Health
summary:
# Summary

Prenatal exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—including bisphenols, phthalates, pesticides, and persistent organic pollutants (POPs)—causes widespread and lasting adverse effects on offspring health through multiple mechanisms. BPA exposure increases odds of lower birth weight and neurobehavioral changes in a dose-dependent manner, while phthalate exposure disrupts immune regulation, metabolic programming, and steroid hormone production, elevating childhood asthma and rhinitis risk by 30–60%. These chemicals mechanistically disrupt endocrine signaling by inhibiting androgen signaling while activating estrogen signaling, simultaneously inducing epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation and histone changes), oxidative stress, and pro-inflammatory responses that alter placental function and nutrient transport. Pesticide exposure, particularly organophosphates and chlorpyrifos, impairs cognitive development and increases insulin resistance, glucose dysmetabolism, and allergic disease susceptibility, whereas POPs and dioxins disrupt thyroid hormone homeostasis and lipid metabolism, collectively increasing childhood obesity risk by up to 22% per doubling of maternal serum concentration. The epigenetic modifications induced by EDCs have far-reaching consequences for immune system regulation, neurodevelopmental processes, metabolic balance, and reproductive health, establishing a mechanistic basis for the long-term programming of chronic disease in offspring exposed prenatally to environmental endocrine disruptors.

title: Plastic
summary: This environmental health study identified multiple classes of plastic additives in menstrual products and assessed their potential health implications, particularly for endometriosis and related gynecological conditions. Researchers detected phthalates, organophosphate esters (OPEs), and alternative plasticizers (APs) across all menstrual product types, with reusable sanitary pads showing the highest concentrations of phthalates and OPEs, while single-use pads contained elevated levels of alternative plasticizers. Because endocrine-disrupting chemicals are associated with endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids, the widespread presence of these additives in menstrual products raises significant health concerns, particularly since dermal exposure through sanitary pads is comparable to dietary phthalate exposure, which is already recognized as a major exposure route. Individual plasticizers including bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), diisononyl phthalate (DiNP), and specific OPEs like tris(2-chloroethyl) phosphate have been linked to carcinogenicity and neurotoxicity in previous studies. The findings underscore the need for regulatory scrutiny of chemical additives in menstrual products, as women's repeated contact with these compounds during reproductive years may constitute a significant and previously underrecognized exposure pathway for endocrine-disrupting substances.

title: Association between exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and reproductive health outcomes: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Health, Population, and Nutrition
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) exposure poses significant risks to reproductive health through multiple mechanisms. PAHs function as endocrine-disrupting chemicals that damage male reproductive function via DNA adduct formation, oxidative stress, and genetic integrity disruption, resulting in reduced semen quality, sperm DNA fragmentation, and decreased testosterone levels. In men, PAH exposure increases the risk of male reproductive cancers by 13%, particularly prostate cancer, and men with idiopathic infertility show substantially elevated concentrations of multiple PAH compounds including fluorene, naphthalene, and benzo(a)pyrene compared to fertile controls. For women, PAH metabolites were associated with infertility, ovulatory dysfunction, and menstrual irregularities, with specific metabolites—particularly 1-OHNAP, 2-OHFLU, and 3-OHFLU—found at significantly higher levels in women with endometriosis than controls. These findings establish PAH exposure as a reproductive health hazard with distinct gender-specific effects, though the associations with female reproductive outcomes show somewhat weaker evidence than those observed in males.

title: Impact of endocrine disruptors on female fertility: an update
summary: Endocrine disruptors (EDs) such as bisphenol A, phthalates, and pesticides impair female fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. These chemicals bind to estrogen, androgen, and progestin receptors while simultaneously disrupting the synthesis of estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone, thereby compromising the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis that controls gonadotropin secretion. The resulting consequences include reduced ovarian reserve, impaired oocyte maturation and quality, altered follicular development, and suppressed ovulation and embryo implantation rates, collectively reducing fertility and in vitro fertilization success. EDs also modulate gene expression through epigenetic changes in DNA methylation and histone structure, producing lasting effects on reproductive function. Additionally, chronic exposure to these ubiquitous environmental chemicals—found in plastics, cosmetics, food packaging, and pesticides—is associated with increased risk of polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and hyperandrogenism-related inflammatory dysregulation. These findings underscore the critical public health impact of environmental chemical exposures on female reproductive health and fertility outcomes.

title: Widespread Multimorbidity in a Cohort of Aging, Radiation-exposed Rhesus Macaques
summary:
# Summary

This study examined aging rhesus macaques exposed to ionizing radiation and identified widespread multimorbidity characterized by accelerated disease onset and reduced lifespan. Radiation exposure caused delayed effects including cardiac complications, insulin resistance, bone loss, hypertension, and deterministic effects such as cataracts and testicular atrophy, with irradiated animals living an average of 5.1 years shorter than controls. The research revealed distinct disease clusters, with diabetes and cataracts emerging as major comorbidity hubs connected to heart disease, tumors, and organ dysfunction, while a separate cluster linked bone loss to female reproductive problems and low body weight. Systemic inflammation and fibrosis—particularly affecting skeletal muscle, liver, kidney, heart, and lung tissues—appear central to the radiation-induced pathology, potentially driving complications such as diabetes, myocarditis, nephropathy, and cancer. Notably, irradiated animals developed 2.9 comorbid conditions on average compared to 1.9 in unexposed controls, demonstrating how radiation accelerates the aging process and generates complex, interconnected disease phenotypes relevant to understanding late effects of radiation exposure.

title: Identifying the known and unknown health hazard information for chemical disasters: a phased scoping review of the East Palestine, Ohio train derailment
journal: Journal of Exposure Science & Environmental Epidemiology
summary:
# Summary

The East Palestine train derailment released multiple hazardous chemicals into the environment, with eight high-priority substances identified as skin and eye irritants, and seven additionally causing respiratory irritation. Key chemicals including vinyl chloride, acrolein, benzene, and acrylate compounds posed significant acute health risks, while volatile organic compounds released during the controlled burn triggered immediate symptoms in community members, first responders, and CDC survey workers—manifesting as headaches, anxiety, coughing, respiratory effects, and nasal and lung irritation. Five of the identified chemicals are known or suspected human or animal carcinogens, and additional compounds including dioxins and PFAS demonstrated immunological effects, while phosgene gas was linked to reproductive and fetal damage at lower evidence levels. This incident exposed the inadequate characterization of health hazards associated with chemical disasters, as substantial gaps exist in toxicity data for many chemicals released during transportation accidents. The exposure patterns differed between affected populations, with residents more commonly reporting systemic symptoms like headaches and anxiety, while first responders experienced greater nasal and lung-specific effects.

title: Serum concentrations of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances and risk of ovarian cancer
journal: JNCI Journal of the National Cancer Institute
summary: This study demonstrates that serum concentrations of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), particularly PFOS and its precursor MeFOSAA, are significantly associated with increased ovarian cancer risk. MeFOSAA showed the strongest association, with women in the highest exposure quartile experiencing 62% greater risk compared to the lowest quartile, and particularly pronounced associations in women aged 55-59 years. PFOS exposure was also associated with elevated ovarian cancer risk, with a 47% increased risk per doubling of serum concentration. Additional PFAS compounds including perfluorononanoic acid and perfluorodecanoic acid showed moderate associations with ovarian cancer, though with wider confidence intervals. The mechanisms underlying these associations likely involve endocrine disruption, as PFAS are known environmental contaminants with hormonal effects. These findings provide novel evidence that PFAS exposure represents an important environmental risk factor for ovarian cancer development, with implications for understanding environmentally-mediated malignancy in reproductive tissues.

title: Biomaterial-Based Hemostasis: A Review of the Clinical and Functional Versatility of Oxidized Regenerated Cellulose
summary: Oxidized regenerated cellulose (ORC) is a polysaccharide-based biomaterial with broad clinical utility across surgical specialties including general, gynecological, orthopedic, and cardiothoracic surgery. The material functions through multiple mechanisms: its oxidation process creates carboxyl groups that form polyuronic acids, which impart hemostatic and bactericidal properties, while the resulting acidic environment promotes vasoconstriction and red blood cell lysis to control bleeding. ORC activates the coagulation cascade and interacts with clotting factors to facilitate thrombus formation without relying on patient coagulation status, making it valuable in anticoagulated patients. Beyond hemostasis, ORC reduces postoperative adhesions in pelvic and abdominal surgeries and inhibits growth of multidrug-resistant organisms including methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*, vancomycin-resistant *Enterococcus*, and *Candida albicans*, thereby reducing surgical site infections. The material converts to a gel within 24 hours and is phagocytosed by macrophages while promoting fibroblast, epithelial, and endothelial cell activity to support tissue repair. Multiple clinical trials demonstrate ORC's efficacy comparable to or equivalent with tranexamic acid, calcium alginate, and other hemostatic agents across diverse surgical applications.

title: Reproductive success in the anthropocene: A view through a One Health lens
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines reproductive health through a One Health lens, demonstrating that anthropogenic activities including industrialization, urbanization, and human population growth have triggered cascading environmental impacts that compromise reproductive success across species. Environmental pollution and climate change directly impair reproductive health by altering ecosystem function and reducing gamete production while increasing structural and functional abnormalities in reproductive cells. The review identifies endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs), microplastics, and persistent organic pollutants as major threats, with EDCs mimicking natural hormones and targeting estrogen and androgen signaling pathways to alter gene expression and epigenetic programming. Early developmental stages, particularly fetal development in utero, show heightened susceptibility to EDC exposure due to immature physiological barriers and rapid growth, with even low-dose prenatal exposure resulting in long-lasting transgenerational reproductive effects. In women, EDC exposure is associated with infertility, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, ovarian dysfunction, and menstrual irregularities, while in men it is linked to infertility, testicular dysfunction, cryptorchidism, hypospadias, testicular cancer, and reduced sperm quality. Collectively, these findings underscore that maintaining reproductive success—essential for species sustainability—requires urgent mitigation of environmental chemical exposures across the global ecosystem.

title: Mycotoxin toxicity and its alleviation strategy on female mammalian reproduction and fertility
summary:
# Summary

Mycotoxins, including zearalenone, ochratoxin A, deoxynivalenol, and aflatoxin B1, represent significant environmental threats to female reproductive health and fertility. These toxins exert widespread effects on oogenesis, follicle assembly, embryo development, and fetal growth while disrupting critical reproductive axes including the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal and gut-ovarian pathways. At the cellular level, mycotoxins impair oocyte maturation by disrupting meiosis resumption, spindle structure, and chromatin organization while simultaneously triggering oxidative stress, genomic instability, and cell cycle arrest. The toxic profile encompasses hepatotoxicity, carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, estrogenic effects, and immunotoxicity, with specific mycotoxins causing documented reproductive pathologies such as endometrial hypertrophy from zearalenone and pup loss with delayed puberty from ochratoxin A exposure. Notably, mycotoxins can cross placental barriers, leading to adverse perinatal outcomes including low birth weight, preterm birth, skeletal and visceral abnormalities, and impaired fetal development.

title: The Human Plastiphere:
summary:
# Summary: Microplastics as a Systemic Human Contaminant

Micro- and nanoplastics (MNPs) have achieved ubiquitous distribution throughout human tissues via three primary exposure routes: pulmonary inhalation (depositing particles in lungs), gastrointestinal ingestion (accumulating in the colon), and dermal contact. The resulting "plastiphere"—a circulating reservoir of synthetic polymers including polyethylene, polypropylene, and PET—has been detected in nearly every major organ system, including blood, brain, heart, reproductive tissues, and placenta, with evidence of transplacental and fetal exposure documented through umbilical cord and amniotic fluid concentrations. Reproductive tissues show particular vulnerability, with testicular burdens reaching 328 μg/g and follicular fluid containing up to 2,191 particles/mL, correlating with reduced sperm motility (8.3% per additional polymer type) and altered hormonal levels (FSH). Beyond distribution, systemic MNP accumulation is mechanistically linked to platelet activation and cardiovascular thrombosis (a 4.5-fold increased risk of myocardial infarction/stroke with polyethylene-containing particles), gut dysbiosis characterized by depletion of protective bacteria (*Faecalibacterium prausnitzii*) and enrichment of pathogenic *Enterobacteriaceae*, and elevated microplastic loads in inflammatory bowel disease and Crohn's disease patients. This comprehensive tissue mapping establishes microplastics as an emerging systemic contaminant with documented pathophysiological associations, particularly relevant to reproductive and gastrointestinal health.

title: A Systematic Review of Nonsugar Sweeteners and Cancer Epidemiology Studies
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluated the epidemiological evidence linking nonsugar sweeteners (NSSs) including aspartame, saccharin, sucralose, acesulfame potassium, and cyclamate to cancer risk across multiple cancer types. Despite numerous observational studies examining associations between NSS consumption and various cancers—including breast, bladder, brain, pancreatic, laryngeal, and hematologic malignancies—the review found no consistent associations between any individual NSS or NSS mixtures and overall cancer risk. Reducing sugar intake conversely showed promise in decreasing both diabetes and cancer risk, representing a potential alternative dietary approach. The authors concluded that experimental animal and mechanistic evidence does not support human-relevant carcinogenicity or genotoxicity from NSSs at current exposure levels. These findings suggest that while NSSs have been used in food and beverage formulations for decades, epidemiological data do not establish a clear causal link between their consumption and cancer development.

title: FIGO committee opinion: Environmental drivers of gynecologic and reproductive health
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This FIGO committee opinion synthesizes evidence on environmental drivers of reproductive and gynecologic health, identifying multiple chemical and physical exposures that impair fertility and reproductive function through distinct biological mechanisms. Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—particularly phthalates and bisphenol A (BPA)—disrupt estrogen and androgen signaling pathways while altering steroid hormone and anti-Müllerian hormone levels, contributing to reduced ovarian reserve and infertility. Air pollution, especially fine particulate matter (PM2.5) and nitrogen dioxide, induces oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to decreased ovarian reserve, reduced antral follicle counts, lower AMH levels, and diminished assisted reproductive technology success rates. Heat stress and elevated ambient temperatures impair ovarian function, reduce menstrual regularity, and are associated with lower clinical pregnancy rates and antral follicle counts. Heavy metals (lead and cadmium) and pesticide exposures cause oxidative damage and reduce semen quality through effects on hormone production and testicular function, while emerging evidence links environmental chemical exposures to gynecologic conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and polycystic ovary syndrome. The underlying mechanisms involve endocrine disruption, oxidative stress, inflammation, and epigenetic modifications affecting reproductive health across the lifespan.

title: Climate Change and Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

Climate change drives global warming and increased air pollution, with far-reaching consequences for reproductive health through multiple pathways including endocrine disruption and inflammatory mechanisms. Air pollutants such as PM2.5, nitrogen dioxide, and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons accumulate in reproductive tissues including germ cells, placental tissue, and ovarian follicular fluid, where they induce oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and DNA damage. These exposures impair fertility, alter fetal development, and compromise pregnancy outcomes through disruption of hormone signaling pathways, particularly estrogen, androgen, progesterone, and thyroid hormone signaling, while also impairing placental development and causing adverse birth outcomes including preterm birth and altered birthweight. Endocrine-disrupting chemicals further accelerate reproductive aging in women and reduce sperm production in men, with specific compounds like bisphenol A adversely affecting ovarian reserve and benzo[a]pyrene altering steroidogenesis. Environmental chemical exposures from climate change thus represent a significant threat to reproductive health across the lifespan through integrated effects on endocrine function, immune competence, and genomic integrity.

title: Endocrine Disruptors and Breast Cancer: A Comprehensive Review
summary: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) including polychlorinated biphenyls, parabens, phenols, and xenoestrogens promote breast cancer through multiple mechanisms involving estrogen receptor binding and hormonal pathway disruption. These chemicals act as agonists or antagonists at estrogen and progesterone receptors, alter hormone synthesis and metabolism, modify hormone receptor expression, and induce epigenetic changes in hormone-responsive cells. Specific compounds like polychlorinated biphenyls activate the aryl hydrocarbon receptor, generate oxidative stress through reactive oxygen species production, and inhibit apoptosis, thereby promoting metastasis and cancer recurrence. A subset of EDCs, particularly polychlorinated biphenyls and parabens, also cause endometriosis and infertility, linking environmental chemical exposure to multiple reproductive pathologies. Additionally, obesogens promote adipogenesis and obesity, which correlates with increased breast cancer risk and worse prognosis in triple-negative tumors. These findings establish EDCs as significant environmental risk factors that disrupt critical tumor suppressor pathways, particularly p53-mediated cell growth control and DNA repair, while simultaneously impairing reproductive health.

title: Dioxins and the One Health Paradigm: An Interdisciplinary Challenge in Environmental Toxicology
summary: Dioxins are highly persistent environmental contaminants generated from multiple anthropogenic sources including waste incineration, industrial processes, combustion, and e-waste recycling, which accumulate in fatty tissues and biomagnify through food chains to reach humans primarily via dietary intake of animal-derived fats. Once released into the environment, dioxins bind strongly to soil and sediments, undergo atmospheric transport, and persist in ecosystems for decades, with emerging concerns that climate change and thawing permafrost may alter their environmental fate and bioavailability. The primary mechanism of dioxin toxicity involves binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor, causing its translocation to the nucleus where it dimerizes with ARNT protein and activates dioxin response elements, triggering diverse toxic effects including immunosuppression, endocrine disruption affecting thyroid, estrogen, androgen, and insulin signaling pathways. Human health effects from dioxin exposure include developmental delays, altered thyroid hormone levels, reproductive problems, metabolic disorders, cognitive and behavioral deficits, and notably, an association with endometriosis, as well as classification as a known human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer. Species-specific vulnerabilities exist, exemplified by marine mammals such as cetaceans that lack functional CYP1A2 enzymes, resulting in prolonged dioxin half-lives and heightened susceptibility to immunotoxicity. Addressing dioxin contamination requires an interdisciplinary One Health approach that considers environmental persistence, bioaccumulation, ecosystem impacts, and the complex interplay between climate change and human health outcomes.

title: Organophosphate pesticide exposure: Demographic and dietary predictors in an urban pregnancy cohort☆
summary:
# Summary

This study examined organophosphate (OP) pesticide exposure in an urban pregnancy cohort by analyzing urinary dialkyl phosphate metabolites, which are byproducts of OP pesticide degradation in the human body. Organophosphate pesticides are well-established neurotoxicants that cross the placental barrier during pregnancy, causing reduced birth weight and neurodevelopmental impairments in exposed fetuses. Dietary intake of fruits, grains, and meat—the primary sources of OP pesticide residues—accounted for the majority of exposure, with fruit and grain consumption showing the strongest associations with urinary metabolite concentrations. Demographic factors including lower pre-pregnancy BMI, Asian ethnicity, employment status, and single marital status were independently associated with higher OP pesticide metabolite levels. Additionally, OP pesticides are reported to influence obesity and type 2 diabetes development, suggesting systemic metabolic effects beyond neurotoxicity. These findings highlight that prenatal OP pesticide exposure operates through multiple demographic and dietary pathways, with important implications for fetal health outcomes during critical developmental windows.

title: Endocrine Disrupting Chemicals and Female Sexual Health: An Emerging Research Priority
summary:
# Summary

Endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—including phthalates, per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), and heavy metals—represent an emerging threat to female sexual health, with approximately 40% of premenopausal women experiencing sexual dysfunction potentially linked to these exposures. EDCs disrupt female sexual function through multiple mechanisms: they reduce key sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone), suppress kisspeptin expression which normally stimulates reproductive hormone release, and decrease vasodilators like nitric oxide needed for genital blood flow and lubrication. At the neurological level, EDCs impair sexual desire and behavior by altering neurotransmitter systems, particularly reducing dopamine while increasing serotonin and glutamate, and by decreasing neuronal plasticity through calcium signaling disruption and oxidative stress. Additionally, EDC exposure increases psychological distress through elevated anxiety, depression, and perceived stress, which further compromise sexual function and orgasmic response. EDCs also contribute to hormone-dependent gynecological conditions—endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and polycystic ovary syndrome—all of which are associated with sexual pain, creating a compounding pathway from environmental chemical exposure to impaired sexual health.

title: Plastic Pollution and Child Health: A Narrative Review of Micro- and Nanoplastics, Additives, and Developmental Risks
summary:
# Summary

Microplastics and nanoplastics, along with their associated chemical additives including bisphenols and phthalates, induce toxic effects in developing organisms through multiple interconnected mechanisms—specifically by promoting oxidative stress, triggering inflammatory cytokine cascades (IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β, TNF), and activating cell death pathways. Evidence demonstrates that prenatal exposure is ubiquitous, with microplastics, nanoplastics, and additives detected in placental tissue, amniotic fluid, cord blood, and meconium, with concentrations highest in formula-fed infants. At the molecular level, plastic exposure generates excessive reactive oxygen species while simultaneously impairing endogenous antioxidant defenses through downregulation of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione, and additionally causes mitochondrial dysfunction and genotoxic damage including telomere shortening and DNA damage. Beyond early developmental toxicity, plastic exposure is associated with multiple long-term health consequences including reduced fertility, impaired fetal growth, altered pubertal development, neurodevelopmental abnormalities, and metabolic disorders such as obesity and insulin resistance. Notably, microplastics also disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, reducing circulating sex hormones and ovarian function, while bisphenols function as endocrine-disrupting chemicals by binding to estrogen receptors. These findings collectively demonstrate that environmental plastic pollution represents a significant and multifaceted threat to child health across reproductive, metabolic, neurological, and immunological systems.

title: Bisphenol A and human fertility: a systematic review
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that bisphenol A (BPA) exerts widespread detrimental effects on human fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. BPA acts as an endocrine disruptor by interacting with estrogen receptors as both an agonist and antagonist, leading to dysregulation of key reproductive hormones including LH, FSH, androgens, and estrogens across the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. In women, BPA exposure compromises ovarian reserve by reducing antral follicle counts and oocyte numbers, while downregulating critical steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, 3β-HSD, CYP19A1) that decrease estradiol and progesterone biosynthesis, ultimately impairing oocyte maturation and IVF outcomes. In men, BPA reduces sperm production and motility through suppression of protein tyrosine phosphorylation, inhibition of Leydig cell proliferation, and induction of oxidative stress via antioxidant enzyme inhibition and lipid peroxidation, resulting in erectile dysfunction and reduced libido. Additionally, BPA exposure during critical developmental windows causes fetal reproductive malformations including hypospadias, cryptorchidism, and testicular abnormalities, while increasing miscarriage and preterm birth risk through disruption of endometrial function and HOXA10 gene expression, and animal models reveal BPA can induce endometriosis-like lesions.

title: Association of in utero exposure to phthalate and DINCH metabolites with placental DNA methylation
summary:
# Summary

In utero exposure to phthalates and DINCH, ubiquitous plastic-derived chemicals found in both maternal and cord blood, is associated with differential DNA methylation patterns in the placenta. These epigenetic alterations affect genes involved in endocrine disruption, metabolism, vascular function, and immune modulation, with associations showing sex-specific and developmental window-dependent effects. Prenatal phthalate and DINCH exposure has been linked to multiple adverse outcomes including pregnancy complications (miscarriage, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes), neonatal consequences (intrauterine growth restriction, preterm birth, low birth weight), and long-term offspring health effects such as obesity, immune disorders, and neurodevelopmental conditions. The identified epigenetic changes involve genes critical for steroid biosynthesis and sex hormone signaling, suggesting that placental DNA methylation alterations may mediate the endocrine-disrupting mechanisms through which these chemicals compromise placental function and fetal development. Mixture analyses confirmed that combined phthalate and DINCH exposure produces consistent directional effects on placental methylation patterns, highlighting the importance of considering cumulative chemical exposure during pregnancy.

title: EndoCompass Project: Environmental Endocrinology
summary:
# Summary

Environmental endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) including pesticides, phthalates, bisphenols, polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs), and polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDEs) disrupt multiple endocrine systems by binding to steroid and thyroid hormone receptors and interfering with hormone biosynthesis. These chemicals target critical neuroendocrine pathways, including gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) neurons, kisspeptin neurons, and the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, leading to altered expression of key reproductive and stress-response hormones. Specific exposures are linked to diverse adverse outcomes including testicular dysgenesis syndrome, male infertility, early puberty, cryptorchidism, intellectual disability, reduced IQ, autism spectrum disorder, obesity, and type 2 diabetes. EDC mixtures containing phthalates, pesticides, and bisphenol A modify expression of corticosterone receptors, stress-response regulatory proteins like Fkbp5, and estrogen receptor-β through epigenetic mechanisms including DNA methylation. The documented decline in age at breast development since the 1970s, particularly in girls with elevated BMI, may reflect cumulative EDC exposure, suggesting that environmental chemical contamination represents a significant public health concern for endocrine-related disorders.

title: Framework to prioritize health outcomes of particulate matter exposure using national claims data
summary: This study develops a comprehensive framework for prioritizing health outcomes associated with particulate matter (PM) exposure using national claims data. Particulate matter causes systemic inflammation by infiltrating circulation and reaching multiple organs, triggering inflammatory responses and oxidative stress that contribute to both acute and chronic disease. The research demonstrates that PM exposure is associated with severe health outcomes including cardiovascular disease, respiratory disease, autoimmune disease, and cancer, as well as increased mortality, hospitalizations, and emergency room visits. Beyond traditional respiratory and cardiovascular endpoints, the study identifies medium-strength associations between PM exposure and diverse conditions including psychiatric, ophthalmologic, dermatologic, otolaryngologic, gynecological, and immunological diseases. Both short-term and long-term PM exposure patterns are linked to disease incidence and progression, with notable findings that cancer incidence specifically correlates with long-term exposure. This framework prioritizes health outcomes by duration of exposure and disease category, providing a systematic approach to quantifying the widespread health burden of air pollution across multiple organ systems and disease domains.

title: Integration of metabolomics and transcriptomics unravels the identification of TPS gene family and functional characterization of a sesquiterpenoid synthesis gene in Curcuma kwangsiensis
summary:
# Summary

This study integrated metabolomics and transcriptomics to characterize the terpene synthase (TPS) gene family in Curcuma kwangsiensis, a medicinal plant traditionally used to treat menstrual disorders and blood stasis. The researchers identified 24 TPS genes (CkTPS1-CkTPS24) and functionally characterized CkTPS10, which catalyzes the production of sesquiterpenoids including α-copaene and farnesol—key bioactive compounds in this plant species. Metabolomic analysis revealed elevated levels of eight sesquiterpenoids (β-selinene, farnesol derivatives, nerolidol, and others) in tuber and rhizome tissues compared to leaves, with these compounds enriched in the sesquiterpenoid and triterpenoid biosynthesis pathway. Gene expression analysis identified 53 differentially expressed genes in the terpenoid backbone biosynthesis pathway and demonstrated positive correlations between eight sesquiterpenoid metabolites and 32 candidate genes, establishing molecular links between gene expression and sesquiterpenoid production. These findings elucidate the biochemical basis for C. kwangsiensis's therapeutic effects on menstrual disorders and provide a comprehensive genetic and metabolic framework for understanding sesquiterpenoid biosynthesis in medicinal plants.

title: Associations of mixed exposure to ambient air pollution and environmental factors with the risk of miscarriage in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study examined how mixed exposures to air pollution and environmental factors affect miscarriage risk in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology. The research identified that fine particulate matter (PM2.5), elevated temperature, and high UV intensity each independently increase miscarriage risk, with combined environmental exposures showing a substantially greater effect (adjusted odds ratio of 3.65). The underlying mechanism appears to involve immune-inflammatory responses, as genetic enrichment analyses revealed that genes associated with both environmental exposures and miscarriage are involved in immune-inflammatory pathways. Notably, several environmental factors including temperature, vegetation greenness (NDVI), UV intensity, and wind speed demonstrated protective effects by mitigating the harmful impacts of air pollutants on reproductive outcomes. The study also highlights that environmental greenness is associated with improved reproductive health markers including androgen and progesterone levels, while air pollutants such as PM2.5, sulfur dioxide, and ozone adversely affect reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that environmental risk stratification and consideration of protective green space exposure may be important for optimizing outcomes in assisted reproductive technology procedures.

title: Slippery Knowledge: Ignorance, Ecologies, and Environment in Endometriosis Framing
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic systemic inflammatory condition with substantial environmental and genetic contributions to its pathogenesis. Environmental exposure to endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs)—including PCBs, dioxins, bisphenol A (BPA), and phthalates—significantly contributes to endometriosis development by activating intracellular signaling pathways associated with proinflammation and altered hormone responsiveness. Notably, dioxin exposure demonstrates transgenerational effects, with children of parents exposed to these toxic agents showing heightened endometriosis risk, mediated through epigenetic changes that persist across multiple generations. The condition manifests clinically through debilitating pain, fatigue, chronic pain, infertility, pain sensitization, and mood disorders, alongside systemic inflammation and altered brain gene expression. Disease risk appears equally attributable to genetic and environmental factors, though exposure to EDCs is disproportionately distributed among people of color, creating notable racial disparities in endometriosis burden and outcomes.

title: Non-Targeted Analysis Workflow of Endocrine-Disrupting
summary:
# Summary

This study employed non-targeted mass spectrometry analysis to identify endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and their metabolites in ovarian follicular fluid from women with reproductive disorders. Ubiquitously found in consumer products, food, beverages, and the environment, EDCs including phthalates, parabens, and phenols accumulate in follicular fluid and cause reproductive dysfunction by directly correlating with reduced female fecundity and diminished ovarian reserve. The analysis detected 14 compounds with high confidence, including parent parabens (methylparaben, ethylparaben, propylparaben) and their phase I and phase II metabolites such as hydroxylated derivatives, alkyl protocatechuates, and sulfate conjugates, revealing that parabens undergo complex biotransformation via hydrolysis, hydroxylation, oxidation, and conjugation pathways. Notably, phase II metabolites including hydroxylated parabens and propylparaben sulfate were identified directly in untreated follicular fluid samples, while aromatic hydroxylation on the paraben ring emerged as a critical metabolic site. These findings underscore the bioaccumulation and metabolic processing of clinically relevant EDCs within the ovarian microenvironment, establishing a direct environmental exposure pathway to reproductive endocrine disruption in women with compromised fertility.

title: Assessment of water pollution and environmental impacts in the Pineios river basin before and after extreme flood events in Thessaly, Greece
journal: Environmental Science and Pollution Research International
summary:
# Summary

The Pineios river basin in Greece exhibits widespread contamination with endocrine-disrupting chemicals and persistent pollutants that pose potential risks for endometriosis and related reproductive health effects. Multiple phthalate esters—including di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), di-n-butyl phthalate, and dimethyl phthalate—were consistently detected in surface waters at concentrations ranging from 0.09–1.89 µg/L, alongside alternative plasticizers and per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) including PFOA and PFOS. These chemicals are known endocrine disruptors and immunotoxicants that accumulate through dietary and drinking water exposure; notably, PFAS-24 and PFOS frequently exceeded EU environmental quality standards, suggesting meaningful human exposure pathways. Sources of contamination include widespread agricultural plastic use, industrial operations, municipal wastewater, and wastewater treatment infrastructure damage, with flood events significantly elevating phthalate levels at estuarine sites. The combination of multiple persistent, bioaccumulative endocrine-disrupting chemicals in this river basin represents a chronic environmental exposure scenario relevant to understanding population-level reproductive and metabolic health risks.

title: Integrated chemical exposome–metabolome profiling of
summary:
# Summary

This integrated chemical exposome-metabolome study reveals that endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) impair human fertility through multiple pathways affecting ovarian function and reproductive outcomes. Exposure to EDC mixtures was associated with 21% fewer retrieved oocytes, demonstrating that cumulative chemical exposures significantly reduce ovarian reserve and oocyte quality. EDCs impair ovarian follicle development, functionality, and hormone signaling while increasing risk of polycystic ovary syndrome and diminished ovarian reserve, and epidemiologic evidence links EDC exposure to oocyte fertilization failure, embryo implantation failure, reduced clinical pregnancy, and lower live birth rates. The study detected multiple classes of pollutants—including phthalates, parabens, phenols, flame retardants, pesticides, and perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS)—directly in follicular fluid, the microenvironment critical for oocyte development. Twenty metabolic pathways involving methionine, nicotinamide, glycine, pyrimidine, selenocompounds, tryptophan, and other biochemical processes showed significant associations with oocyte count, suggesting chemical exposures disrupt fundamental metabolic processes required for ovarian function. Specific PFAS compounds found in every follicular fluid sample were associated with lower embryo quality, diminished ovarian reserve, reduced fertilization rates, and decreased pregnancy odds, highlighting persistent bioaccumulative chemicals as particular threats to reproductive health.

title: Impact of Essential and Toxic Trace Elements on Cervical Premalignant Lesions
summary:
# Summary

Human papillomavirus infection, particularly HPV-16 and HPV-18 strains, causes cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN), which progresses to cervical cancer if untreated, though conization reduces cancer risk by 95%. This study examined how essential and toxic trace elements influence CIN pathogenesis, finding that CIN tissues exhibited significantly elevated concentrations of multiple elements including chromium, cobalt, selenium, molybdenum, nickel, beryllium, and cadmium compared to normal cervical tissue. While essential trace elements like zinc and selenium normally support DNA repair and antioxidative defense, their dysregulation combined with toxic element accumulation—particularly nickel, cadmium, and beryllium—promotes carcinogenesis through mechanisms including oxidative stress, DNA damage, hypoxia-induced cell proliferation, and endocrine disruption. The research suggests that dysregulation of trace element homeostasis, alongside HPV infection, contributes to cervical neoplasm development and indicates a complex interaction between environmental chemical exposures and reproductive tract malignancy risk.

title: Metabolomics of Prostate Cancer and Clinical Profiles Following Radiotherapy: Need for a Precision Phylometabolomics Approach †
summary:
# Metabolomics of Prostate Cancer Following Radiotherapy

This study applied parsimony phylogenetic analysis to serum metabolomics data from prostate cancer patients undergoing stereotactic body radiation therapy, identifying five distinct phylogenetic subgroups with unique metabolomic profiles that did not correlate with PSA levels. Radiotherapy induced significant alterations in multiple metabolic pathways including nitrogen, pyrimidine, purine, porphyrin, alanine, aspartate, glutamate, and glycerophospholipid metabolism, with 88 pre-radiotherapy and 29 post-radiotherapy metabolic features showing aberrations. Patients who subsequently developed metastasis exhibited characteristic alterations in specific metabolites including D-tryptophan, hypoxanthine, bilirubin, phosphatidylcholine, and 5′-benzoylphosphoadenosine, with hypoxanthine and phthalic acid present in all high-risk patients. The combination of androgen deprivation therapy with radiotherapy improved metastasis-free survival and patient outcomes through mechanisms including reduced DNA repair protein expression and enhanced immune infiltration. This precision phylometabolomics approach provides superior stratification of cancer patients compared to traditional biomarkers like PSA and offers potential for developing targeted, personalized treatment strategies based on individual metabolic signatures.

title: The effects of endocrine disruptors on the female reproductive system
summary:
# Summary

Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) including bisphenol A, phthalates, and dioxins cause widespread harm to female reproductive health by disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and impairing gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion. These exposures are strongly associated with endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, infertility, menstrual cycle disruption, and increased risk of hormone-sensitive cancers and uterine dysfunction. Bisphenol A specifically reduces blood estradiol levels and follicle development, leading to follicular atresia and ovarian failure, while also crossing the placental barrier to affect fetal development. Phthalate metabolites are markedly elevated in women with endometriosis and stimulate endometrial stromal cell proliferation. Emerging concerns include micro- and nanoplastics, which accumulate in uterine tissue and developing follicles while reducing anti-Müllerian hormone and estradiol levels, suggesting that plastic contamination poses an additional environmental threat to reproductive function.

title: Endocrine disruptors and children’s health
summary:
# Summary

Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) comprise a diverse class of environmental contaminants—including bisphenol A, phthalates, polychlorinated biphenyls, and per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances—that disrupt hormonal signaling by activating estrogen receptors and inhibiting androgen receptors, with widespread exposure occurring through plastics, cosmetics, thermal paper receipts, and breast milk. Prenatal and early-life EDC exposure is causally linked to multiple adverse outcomes in children, including neurodevelopmental delays, reproductive dysfunction, obesity, type 2 diabetes, thyroid dysfunction, preterm birth, and low birth weight, with specific compounds like BPA disrupting synaptic plasticity through alterations in GABA and glutamate levels. Many EDCs impair thyroid hormone homeostasis through multiple mechanisms—including inhibition of iodide transporters, disruption of thyroid hormone transport proteins, and direct effects on thyroid hormone metabolism—resulting in reduced cognitive performance, attention disorders, and intellectual disability. Epigenetic mechanisms including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and microRNA expression mediate the transgenerational effects of EDC exposure.
